distractor_explanations.C: Peritonsillar abscess (Quinsy) is a suppurative complication of streptococcal pharyngitis. Prompt antibiotic therapy reduces the risk of suppurative complications like abscesses, cellulitis, and mastoiditis.
distractor_explanations.D: Sydenham chorea is one of the major clinical manifestations (Jones criteria) of acute rheumatic fever. Because antibiotics prevent ARF, they also prevent the development of Sydenham chorea.
distractor_explanations.E: Scarlet fever is an acute, toxin-mediated manifestation of a GAS infection characterized by a sandpaper-like rash and strawberry tongue. It occurs during the acute phase of the infection rather than as a delayed sequela like PSGN.

learning_objective: Recognize that antibiotic treatment of Group A Streptococcus pharyngitis prevents acute rheumatic fever and suppurative complications but does not prevent the development of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
key_concept: The disconnect between antibiotic efficacy in preventing acute rheumatic fever versus post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
subject: Pathology

vignette: A 64-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a "heavy sensation" in his left scrotum for the past two months and one recent episode of painless gross hematuria. He has a 45-pack-year smoking history and well-controlled hypertension. On physical examination, a 3-cm, firm, non-tender mass is palpable in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen. Scrotal examination reveals a cluster of dilated veins above the left testicle that feels like a "bag of worms." This scrotal mass remains prominent and does not decrease in size when the patient moves from a standing to a supine position. The right scrotum is normal.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to evaluate for the suspected underlying cause of this patient's scrotal findings?

answer_choices.A: Scrotal ultrasound
answer_choices.C: Cystoscopy
answer_choices.D: Serum prostate-specific antigen
answer_choices.E: Reassurance and scrotal support

explanation: The patient presents with several classic signs of Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC): smoking history, hematuria, a palpable flank mass, and a left-sided varicocele. A key clinical pearl for the USMLE is that a left-sided varicocele that does not decompress when the patient lies flat (non-reducing) indicates a secondary varicocele. This occurs because the left gonadal vein drains into the left renal vein; a tumor (like RCC) invading or obstructing the left renal vein prevents venous drainage from the scrotum. The most appropriate next step to evaluate this suspected malignancy is a CT scan of the abdomen (or MRI) to visualize the kidney and the extent of any renal vein or IVC involvement. While only 10% of patients present with the classic triad of hematuria, flank pain, and palpable mass, the presence of a non-reducing varicocele in an older smoker is highly suggestive of RCC.

distractor_explanations.A: Scrotal ultrasound is useful for evaluating primary varicoceles or testicular masses. However, in this patient, the varicocele is a secondary finding caused by proximal obstruction (renal vein). Evaluating the kidney (CT abdomen) is the priority.
distractor_explanations.C: Cystoscopy is used to evaluate the bladder and ureters for sources of hematuria (e.g., bladder cancer). While it may be performed during a hematuria workup, it will not diagnose the renal mass or explain the non-reducing left-sided varicocele.
distractor_explanations.D: Serum prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a screening tool for prostate cancer, which usually presents with obstructive voiding symptoms or bone pain, not a flank mass and varicocele.
distractor_explanations.E: Reassurance and scrotal support are appropriate for asymptomatic, primary (idiopathic) varicoceles that reduce when supine. A non-reducing varicocele in an older man requires urgent investigation for underlying malignancy.

learning_objective: A left-sided varicocele that fails to decompress in the supine position in an older patient should raise immediate suspicion for renal cell carcinoma obstructing the renal vein.
key_concept: Secondary varicocele as a sign of renal vein obstruction by RCC.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old male smoker with a 50 pack-year history presents to a community health center with a 3-month history of dull left-sided flank pain and two episodes of painless gross hematuria. He also reports unintentional weight loss of 12 lbs. Physical examination reveals a left-sided scrotal mass that feels like a 'bag of worms' and does not decrease in size when the patient is in the supine position. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 19.1 g/dL, a corrected calcium of 11.2 mg/dL, and an alkaline phosphatase of 260 U/L (normal: 30–120 U/L). Serum bilirubin and transaminases are within normal limits. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis shows an 8.2-cm heterogeneously enhancing mass in the lower pole of the left kidney with a tumor thrombus extending into the left renal vein. A CT of the chest shows no evidence of pulmonary nodules or lymphadenopathy.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Percutaneous needle biopsy of the renal mass
answer_choices.C: Left-sided testicular ultrasound with Doppler
answer_choices.D: Immediate radical nephrectomy
answer_choices.E: Initiation of sunitinib therapy

explanation: This patient presents with classic features of Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC): hematuria, flank pain, weight loss, and smoking history. The presence of a non-reducing left-sided varicocele is a high-yield physical exam finding suggesting that the tumor has invaded the left renal vein, thereby obstructing the drainage of the left gonadal vein. The laboratory findings illustrate multiple paraneoplastic syndromes: polycythemia (due to ectopic erythropoietin production), hypercalcemia (likely due to PTHrP), and elevated alkaline phosphatase. In the context of RCC, an elevated alkaline phosphatase can represent either Stauffer syndrome (paraneoplastic hepatic dysfunction without metastases, often IL-6 mediated) or bone metastases. According to standard staging protocols, if a patient with suspected RCC has an elevated alkaline phosphatase or bone pain, a radionuclide bone scan is the next best step to evaluate for skeletal metastases. Completion of staging is essential before determining if the patient should undergo radical nephrectomy with curative intent or if the approach should shift toward palliative or systemic therapies.

distractor_explanations.A: Percutaneous biopsy of a renal mass is generally avoided if the imaging is highly suggestive of RCC, as it carries a risk of tract seeding and often does not change the management (the mass is surgically removed regardless). Biopsy is reserved for cases where an alternative diagnosis like lymphoma or infection is suspected, or for tissue diagnosis in metastatic disease.
distractor_explanations.C: While the patient has a varicocele, the diagnosis is clinically evident and the underlying cause (renal vein obstruction by the renal mass) has already been identified on the abdominal CT. A testicular ultrasound would not change management or contribute to the staging of the primary malignancy.
distractor_explanations.D: Radical nephrectomy is the definitive treatment for localized RCC. However, the alkaline phosphatase is significantly elevated, and staging must be completed (ruling out bone metastases via bone scan) before performing major surgery with curative intent.
distractor_explanations.E: Sunitinib (a VEGF/PDGF receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor) is used for metastatic RCC. While the patient has systemic symptoms, metastatic disease has not yet been confirmed. Staging must be completed first.

learning_objective: In patients with suspected renal cell carcinoma and an elevated alkaline phosphatase, a radionuclide bone scan is required to rule out skeletal metastases as part of the initial staging workup.
key_concept: RCC paraneoplastic syndromes (polycythemia, hypercalcemia, Stauffer syndrome) and the necessity of complete staging (including bone scan for elevated Alk Phos) prior to surgical intervention.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old female presents to a community health center reporting a 4-month history of progressive fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 12 lbs. She has a 25 pack-year smoking history and medically managed hypertension. On physical examination, she appears pale. Abdominal palpation reveals a firm, non-tender mass in the right upper quadrant that moves with respiration. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 17.8 g/dL, serum calcium of 10.9 mg/dL, and an alkaline phosphatase of 280 U/L (normal: 44–147 U/L). Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) are mildly elevated at 55 U/L and 62 U/L, respectively. Total bilirubin is within normal limits, but the prothrombin time is slightly prolonged. A CT scan of the abdomen reveals a 7.5 cm heterogeneously enhancing mass in the upper pole of the right kidney. The liver appears normal in size and contour with no focal lesions or intrahepatic ductal dilation. There is no evidence of lymphadenopathy or venous thrombus.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's liver laboratory abnormalities?

answer_choices.A: Direct malignant invasion of the hepatic parenchyma
answer_choices.C: Obstruction of the inferior vena cava by tumor thrombus
answer_choices.D: Secondary polycythemia-induced hepatic congestion
answer_choices.E: Autoimmune destruction of the intrahepatic bile ducts

explanation: This patient presents with a classic constellation of findings for Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC), including a flank mass, systemic symptoms (weight loss, night sweats), smoking history, and paraneoplastic syndromes (polycythemia due to erythropoietin production and hypercalcemia due to PTHrP). The elevation in liver enzymes (specifically alkaline phosphatase) and prolonged prothrombin time in the absence of hepatic metastases on CT imaging is characteristic of Stauffer syndrome. Stauffer syndrome is a paraneoplastic manifestation of RCC characterized by reversible hepatic dysfunction. The pathophysiology is thought to be mediated by systemic inflammatory cytokines, most notably interleukin-6 (IL-6). These abnormalities typically resolve following a nephrectomy (removal of the primary tumor).

distractor_explanations.A: While the liver is a common site for RCC metastasis, the CT scan in this patient specifically showed no focal liver lesions, making direct invasion or metastasis unlikely as the cause of the LFT abnormalities.
distractor_explanations.C: RCC can invade the renal vein and extend into the inferior vena cava (IVC), potentially causing Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic vein obstruction). However, the CT scan showed no evidence of venous thrombus, and Budd-Chiari usually presents with hepatomegaly and ascites.
distractor_explanations.D: While the patient has polycythemia (likely from paraneoplastic EPO production), polycythemia itself does not typically cause isolated alkaline phosphatase elevation and prolonged PT without causing overt hyperviscosity or thrombosis (e.g., Budd-Chiari), which were not present here.
distractor_explanations.E: Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) causes elevated alkaline phosphatase and is more common in middle-aged women, but it does not explain the large renal mass, polycythemia, or systemic 'B' symptoms found in this patient.

learning_objective: Recognize Stauffer syndrome as a paraneoplastic manifestation of renal cell carcinoma characterized by reversible hepatic dysfunction (e.g., elevated alkaline phosphatase, prolonged PT) in the absence of liver metastases.
key_concept: Stauffer syndrome (non-metastatic hepatic dysfunction) in the setting of Renal Cell Carcinoma.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old female presents to an urgent care center complaining of a 3-week history of fatigue and a persistent cough. She has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, which led to end-stage renal disease. She has been receiving hemodialysis for the past seven years. During the evaluation of her cough, a chest X-ray is clear, but she mentions occasional dark-tinged urine. Physical examination is notable only for a right-sided arteriovenous fistula and dry mucous membranes. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 17.2 g/dL, which is a significant increase from her baseline of 11.8 g/dL six months ago. A follow-up CT scan of the abdomen reveals bilateral shrunken kidneys with multiple small cysts and a 4.2-cm heterogeneously enhancing mass in the upper pole of the left kidney. Biopsy of the mass confirms clear cell renal cell carcinoma.
question_stem: Which of the following best describes the molecular mechanism responsible for this patient's elevated hemoglobin level?

answer_choices.A: Constitutive activation of a receptor tyrosine kinase via chromosomal translocation
answer_choices.B: Inhibition of a phosphatase that normally antagonizes the PI3K/Akt pathway
answer_choices.C: Increased stability of a transcription factor normally targeted for proteasomal degradation
answer_choices.D: Epigenetic silencing of a gene responsible for DNA mismatch repair
answer_choices.E: Gain-of-function mutation in a cytoplasmic tyrosine kinase involved in JAK-STAT signaling

explanation: This patient has Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma (RCC), likely arising in the setting of Acquired Cystic Kidney Disease (ACKD) due to long-term dialysis (dialysis >3-5 years significantly increases RCC risk). The patient also exhibits paraneoplastic polycythemia, caused by the ectopic production of erythropoietin (EPO). The molecular driver of clear cell RCC involves the loss of the von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) tumor suppressor protein on chromosome 3p. In healthy cells, VHL protein is part of an E3 ubiquitin ligase complex that targets Hypoxia-Inducible Factor 1-alpha (HIF-1α) for proteasomal degradation under normoxic conditions. When VHL is inactivated (as in 90% of sporadic clear cell RCC), HIF-1α accumulates and acts as a transcription factor for various genes, including vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) for angiogenesis, platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), and erythropoietin (EPO). The inappropriate accumulation of HIF-1α leads to the high vascularity of these tumors and paraneoplastic syndromes like polycythemia.

distractor_explanations.A: This describes the mechanism of some papillary RCCs (MET proto-oncogene) or certain leukemias (BCR-ABL). While papillary RCC is the second most common type, it is not the 'clear cell' type mentioned and does not primarily involve the VHL/HIF pathway.
distractor_explanations.B: This describes the loss of PTEN (as seen in Cowden syndrome or some prostate/endometrial cancers). While the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway is often upregulated downstream in RCC, the primary driver for the paraneoplastic EPO production is the HIF pathway.
distractor_explanations.C: This is the correct mechanism: loss of VHL leads to the failure to ubiquitinate HIF-1α, causing its stability and accumulation, which drives EPO production.
distractor_explanations.D: This describes the mechanism behind Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer), which is not the primary driver of renal cell carcinoma.
distractor_explanations.E: This describes the JAK2 V617F mutation found in Polycythemia Vera. While Polycythemia Vera also presents with high hemoglobin, this patient’s polycythemia is a paraneoplastic effect of her renal mass.

learning_objective: Understand that the molecular hallmark of clear cell renal cell carcinoma is the inactivation of the VHL gene, leading to the accumulation of Hypoxia-Inducible Factor (HIF) and subsequent production of VEGF and erythropoietin.
key_concept: The VHL-HIF-VEGF/EPO pathway in clear cell renal cell carcinoma and its association with acquired cystic kidney disease in dialysis patients.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with a three-month history of intermittent, painless gross hematuria and a dull, aching sensation in his left flank. He has a 50 pack-year smoking history and well-controlled hypertension. On physical examination, a firm, non-tender mass is palpable in the left upper quadrant. A left-sided varicocele is noted that remains prominent and does not decompress when the patient transitions from a standing to a supine position. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin of 18.8 g/dL, serum calcium of 11.1 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast reveals a 6.2-cm, heterogeneously enhancing solid mass in the lower pole of the left kidney. There is no evidence of tumor extension into the renal vein or local lymphadenopathy. The right kidney and liver appear normal.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: CT scan of the chest
answer_choices.B: Percutaneous fine-needle aspiration biopsy of the mass
answer_choices.C: Radical nephrectomy
answer_choices.D: Serum erythropoietin level
answer_choices.E: Technetium-99m bone scintigraphy

explanation: The patient presents with the classic triad of renal cell carcinoma (RCC): hematuria, flank pain, and a palpable abdominal mass (found in only 10% of cases). The non-reducible left-sided varicocele is highly suggestive of RCC obstructing the left renal vein, where the left gonadal vein normally drains. The laboratory findings (polycythemia and hypercalcemia) are classic paraneoplastic syndromes of RCC (due to ectopic production of erythropoietin and PTHrP, respectively). Once an enhancing renal mass is identified on CT abdomen/pelvis, the most appropriate next step is to complete the staging workup. Because the lung is the most common site of distant metastasis for RCC, a CT scan of the chest is required to rule out pulmonary involvement before definitive surgical planning. Even if a nephrectomy is eventually indicated, the procedure cannot be appropriately planned or the prognosis determined without full staging (TNM).

distractor_explanations.A: This is the correct next step. RCC staging requires CT chest because of the high frequency of lung metastases.
distractor_explanations.B: Renal mass biopsy is generally avoided in patients with a classic presentation and enhancing solid mass because it carries a risk of tract seeding and often does not change management (the diagnosis is typically confirmed via surgical pathology).
distractor_explanations.C: While radical nephrectomy is the likely definitive treatment for a 6.2-cm (T1b) mass in a patient with a healthy contralateral kidney, staging must be completed first with chest imaging to ensure there is no metastatic disease that would change the management strategy.
distractor_explanations.D: While the patient's polycythemia is likely due to ectopic erythropoietin (EPO) production by the tumor, measuring the serum EPO level is not necessary and does not change the clinical management or staging.
distractor_explanations.E: Bone scintigraphy is not part of the routine staging for RCC unless the patient has symptoms of bone pain or an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, neither of which is present here.

learning_objective: After identifying an enhancing renal mass suspicious for renal cell carcinoma on abdominal imaging, the next best step in management is staging with a CT scan of the chest to rule out pulmonary metastasis.
key_concept: Staging requirements for Renal Cell Carcinoma
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 3-month-old female, born at 26 weeks gestation, is brought to the urgent care clinic by her mother for extreme fussiness and two episodes of non-bilious vomiting. The mother reports the infant has been 'screaming in cycles' and had only one wet diaper in the last 12 hours. The infant's medical history is significant for respiratory distress syndrome and a prolonged NICU stay, during which she received several weeks of furosemide therapy. Physical examination shows a temperature of 39.0°C (102.2°F), heart rate of 170/min, and a fussy infant who is difficult to console. Urinalysis reveals 2+ blood, positive nitrites, and >50 WBCs per high-power field. A renal ultrasound demonstrates a 7-mm echogenic focus with shadowing at the left vesicoureteral junction, associated with moderate hydroureter and hydronephrosis.
question_stem: Which of the following is the next best step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy
answer_choices.B: Urgent surgical decompression of the collecting system
answer_choices.C: Medical expulsive therapy with an alpha-blocker
answer_choices.D: 24-hour urine collection for metabolic evaluation
answer_choices.E: Non-contrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

explanation: This infant is presenting with an infected, obstructed kidney (obstructive pyelonephritis), which is a urological emergency. The history of prematurity and furosemide use is a known risk factor for pediatric nephrolithiasis (due to furosemide-induced hypercalciuria). The presence of a 7-mm obstructing stone (demonstrated on ultrasound as hydronephrosis and hydroureter) combined with signs of systemic infection (fever, positive nitrites, and pyuria) indicates that the 'pus is under pressure.' Antibiotics alone cannot effectively penetrate an obstructed system, and the patient is at high risk for rapid progression to urosepsis and permanent renal scarring. The next best step is urgent decompression of the urinary collecting system, typically via a percutaneous nephrostomy tube or a retrograde ureteral stent.

distractor_explanations.A: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) is a treatment for certain renal and ureteral stones, but it is contraindicated in the setting of an acute urinary tract infection. Decompression must occur first.
distractor_explanations.C: Medical expulsive therapy (e.g., tamsulosin) is used for small, uncomplicated distal ureteral stones in adults to facilitate passage. It is not appropriate for an obstructed system with systemic signs of infection, and its safety/efficacy in 3-month-old infants is not standard of care for acute management.
distractor_explanations.D: A 24-hour urine collection is part of the long-term metabolic workup for recurrent stone formers to identify underlying causes (e.g., hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria). While indicated later for this patient given her age, it is not the next step in the management of an acute, life-threatening complication.
distractor_explanations.E: In children, renal ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality for suspected nephrolithiasis to avoid ionizing radiation. Since the diagnosis of an obstructing stone and hydronephrosis has already been established by ultrasound, a CT scan is unnecessary and would delay life-saving treatment.

learning_objective: Recognize that the combination of urinary tract infection and ureteral obstruction (infected nephrolithiasis) is a surgical emergency requiring urgent decompression.
key_concept: The management of an obstructing stone in the setting of infection requires urgent surgical decompression (stent or nephrostomy) and IV antibiotics.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old male is currently in the intensive care unit on postoperative day 5 following an emergent exploratory laparotomy and extensive small bowel resection (approximately 60 cm of terminal ileum) due to a perforated segment of Crohn's disease. He has started receiving total parenteral nutrition. Suddenly, he develops excruciating, colicky right-sided flank pain that radiates to the groin, associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. He is writhing in bed and cannot find a comfortable position. Physical examination shows significant right-sided costovertebral angle tenderness. A urinalysis reveals 2+ blood and microscopic evaluation shows envelope-shaped crystals but no leukocytes or nitrites. A non-contrast CT abdomen and pelvis demonstrates a 4 mm radiopaque stone in the right ureterovesical junction with mild hydroureter. The patient reports a history of two similar episodes in the past three years. After providing acute pain management and IV fluids, the medical team discusses long-term management to prevent recurrence.
question_stem: Which of the following recommendations is most appropriate for the long-term prevention of stone recurrence in this patient?

answer_choices.A: Decrease dietary calcium intake
answer_choices.C: Initiate hydrochlorothiazide therapy
answer_choices.D: Initiate allopurinol therapy
answer_choices.E: Decrease dietary protein intake

explanation: This patient has nephrolithiasis caused by enteric hyperoxaluria, a frequent complication of Crohn's disease and ileal resection. Under normal physiological conditions, dietary calcium binds to dietary oxalate in the intestinal lumen, forming an insoluble complex that is excreted in the feces. However, in patients with fat malabsorption (due to ileal resection or Crohn's), unabsorbed fatty acids bind to calcium (saponification). This leaves oxalate 'free' and unbound in the gut, where it is readily absorbed into the bloodstream and then excreted by the kidneys. This leads to supersaturation of calcium oxalate in the urine. To prevent this, patients are advised to increase dietary calcium intake (or take calcium supplements with meals) so that enough calcium is available to bind the oxalate despite the presence of malabsorbed fats. Additionally, a low-fat and low-oxalate diet is recommended.

distractor_explanations.A: Decreasing dietary calcium intake is a common misconception; it actually increases the risk of calcium oxalate stones because less calcium is available in the gut to bind oxalate, leading to increased oxalate absorption and urinary excretion.
distractor_explanations.C: Thiazide diuretics (e.g., hydrochlorothiazide) are used to prevent stones in patients with idiopathic hypercalciuria by increasing calcium reabsorption in the distal tubule. In enteric hyperoxaluria, urinary calcium is often low or normal, and the primary driver is oxalate, so thiazides are not the first-line preventive measure.
distractor_explanations.D: Allopurinol is used to prevent uric acid stones (by decreasing uric acid production) or in patients with calcium stones who have documented hyperuricosuria. This patient's clinical history and crystal morphology (envelope-shaped calcium oxalate) point to hyperoxaluria.
distractor_explanations.E: While a low-protein and low-sodium diet can help reduce the risk of calcium stones in some populations, the most specific and effective intervention for stone prevention in the setting of malabsorption is addressing the calcium-oxalate imbalance in the gut.

learning_objective: Recognize that patients with fat malabsorption (e.g., Crohn's disease, ileal resection) are at high risk for calcium oxalate stones due to enteric hyperoxaluria, and that prevention involves increasing dietary calcium to bind intestinal oxalate.
key_concept: Enteric hyperoxaluria pathophysiology and prevention
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 67-year-old female is admitted to the intensive care unit for management of septic shock. She presented with a 24-hour history of severe, colicky right flank pain that radiates to her labia, accompanied by nausea and shaking chills. Her temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), blood pressure is 86/52 mmHg, and heart rate is 118/min. Physical examination reveals a patient writhing in discomfort with significant right-sided costovertebral angle tenderness. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 21,000/mm³ and a serum creatinine of 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL). A non-contrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis shows a 12 mm stone in the right proximal ureter with marked hydronephrosis and perinephric fat stranding. She is started on aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the surgical management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
answer_choices.C: Ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy and stone extraction
answer_choices.D: Tamsulosin therapy and observation for spontaneous passage
answer_choices.E: Open ureterolithotomy

explanation: This patient presents with the classic triad of an obstructed infected kidney: nephrolithiasis, evidence of infection (fever, leukocytosis), and evidence of obstruction (hydronephrosis, AKI). In the setting of urosepsis due to an obstructing stone, the most critical management step is urgent decompression of the collecting system. This is typically achieved via percutaneous nephrostomy or the retrograde placement of a ureteral stent. The pathophysiology of this emergency involves a 'closed-space' infection; as intrapelvic pressure increases behind the obstruction, bacteria and endotoxins are forced into the renal venous and lymphatic systems (pyelovenous backflow), leading to rapid systemic sepsis. Definitive stone treatment is strictly contraindicated until the infection has cleared and the patient is hemodynamically stable.

distractor_explanations.A: ESWL is a common treatment for renal and proximal ureteral stones, but it is contraindicated in the setting of an active urinary tract infection or sepsis, as the shock waves can further disseminate bacteria into the bloodstream.
distractor_explanations.C: While ureteroscopy is a definitive treatment for stones of this size, it should be avoided in the acute phase of urosepsis. Instrumentation of an infected, obstructed system can worsen the bacteremia. The priority is drainage, not stone removal.
distractor_explanations.D: Tamsulosin (medical expulsive therapy) is used for stones <10 mm in stable patients. This stone is >10 mm and is complicated by sepsis and AKI, which are absolute indications for urgent procedural intervention rather than observation.
distractor_explanations.E: Open ureterolithotomy is rarely performed today due to the success of minimally invasive techniques (ureteroscopy, PCNL). Even if indicated, it would not be performed in a hemodynamically unstable septic patient.

learning_objective: Recognize that an obstructed, infected kidney is a surgical emergency requiring immediate decompression with a ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy.
key_concept: Management of infected nephrolithiasis requires urgent drainage of the collecting system to prevent worsening urosepsis and renal damage.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old male is brought to a community health center by his wife due to sudden onset, severe right-sided flank pain that began three hours ago. The pain is colicky, radiates to his right groin, and is associated with nausea and two episodes of non-bilious emesis. On examination, the patient is visibly distressed and writhing on the stretcher, unable to find a comfortable position. He has significant right-costovertebral angle tenderness. His temperature is 38.9°C (102.1°F), heart rate is 112/min, and blood pressure is 106/68 mmHg. Urinalysis reveals gross hematuria, 50-100 WBCs/hpf, and many bacteria. A non-contrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis confirms a 6-mm stone in the right proximal ureter with moderate proximal hydronephrosis.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Prescription for tamsulosin and outpatient urology follow-up in 48 hours
answer_choices.B: Urgent surgical decompression of the renal collecting system
answer_choices.C: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL)
answer_choices.D: Increased oral fluid intake and strain all urine at home
answer_choices.E: Intravenous ketorolac and discharge with a 10-day course of ciprofloxacin

explanation: This patient presents with a classic case of nephrolithiasis (colicky flank pain, hematuria, writhing in pain) complicated by an infection (fever, tachycardia, pyuria, and bacteriuria). In the setting of an obstructed collecting system (hydronephrosis from a 6-mm stone), a concurrent urinary tract infection is a urologic emergency. The 'infected stone' can lead to rapid development of urosepsis, pyonephrosis, and renal failure. The most important patient safety intervention is urgent decompression of the obstructed kidney. This is typically achieved via a percutaneous nephrostomy tube or retrograde ureteral stent placement. Definitive stone removal (e.g., lithotripsy) is deferred until the infection has cleared and the patient is stable.

distractor_explanations.A: While tamsulosin is used as medical expulsive therapy for stones <10mm, it is only appropriate for uncomplicated stones. The presence of fever and signs of infection make outpatient management unsafe.
distractor_explanations.C: ESWL is a treatment for stone fragmentation but is contraindicated in the acute phase of an infected, obstructed kidney. Decompression must occur first to allow drainage of infected urine.
distractor_explanations.D: Conservative management (fluids and straining urine) is appropriate for stones <5mm that are uncomplicated. This patient has a 6-mm stone and systemic signs of infection, requiring urgent intervention.
distractor_explanations.E: Ketorolac is an excellent analgesic for renal colic, and antibiotics are necessary; however, discharging a patient with an infected, obstructed kidney is a major safety error. Antibiotics cannot effectively penetrate an obstructed, infected collecting system without decompression.

learning_objective: Recognize that nephrolithiasis associated with infection, sepsis, or acute kidney injury requires urgent surgical decompression of the urinary tract.
key_concept: An obstructed urinary system in the setting of infection is a surgical emergency requiring immediate decompression (stent or nephrostomy).
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 22-year-old woman presents to an urgent care clinic with a 3-month history of frequent burning retrosternal chest pain. The pain typically occurs 30 to 60 minutes after eating, particularly after she consumes large meals or spicy foods. She notes that the symptoms are significantly worse at night when she lies down to sleep and are occasionally accompanied by a sour taste in the back of her throat. She has no difficulty swallowing, no unintentional weight loss, and no history of tobacco use. Her BMI is 27 kg/m². Physical examination is unremarkable, and an electrocardiogram performed in the clinic shows a normal sinus rhythm with no ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most likely underlying pathophysiology of this patient's symptoms?

answer_choices.A: Failure of the lower esophageal sphincter to relax during deglutition
answer_choices.B: Inappropriate transient relaxations of the lower esophageal sphincter
answer_choices.C: Autoimmune-mediated destruction of gastric parietal cells
answer_choices.D: Outpouching of the esophageal mucosa through Killian's triangle
answer_choices.E: Eosinophilic infiltration of the esophageal mucosa

explanation: The patient's clinical presentation is classic for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), characterized by heartburn and acid regurgitation exacerbated by large meals and a recumbent position. In the majority of patients with GERD, especially those without significant anatomical abnormalities like a large hiatal hernia, the primary underlying mechanism is transient relaxations of the lower esophageal sphincter (TLESRs). TLESRs are physiological events that allow gas to escape the stomach (belching) but occur more frequently or inappropriately in patients with GERD, allowing gastric acid to reflux into the esophagus and cause mucosal irritation.

distractor_explanations.A: Failure of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to relax is the hallmark of achalasia, which typically presents with progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids and regurgitation of undigested food, rather than burning retrosternal pain triggered by meals.
distractor_explanations.C: Autoimmune destruction of parietal cells leads to chronic atrophic gastritis and pernicious anemia. This condition results in achlorhydria (lack of acid production) rather than acid reflux.
distractor_explanations.D: Outpouching through Killian's triangle describes a Zenker diverticulum. This typically presents in older patients with halitosis, regurgitation of food particles hours after eating, and occasionally a neck mass, rather than substernal burning.
distractor_explanations.E: Eosinophilic esophagitis is characterized by eosinophilic infiltration of the esophageal mucosa. It typically presents in younger patients with a history of atopy (asthma, allergies) and is most commonly associated with solid-food dysphagia or food impaction.

learning_objective: Recognize that the primary pathophysiological mechanism of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is transient relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.
key_concept: The most common cause of GERD is inappropriate transient relaxations of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), which allows gastric contents to enter the esophagus.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old Caucasian female presents to the community health center for a routine follow-up regarding gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). She has experienced frequent heartburn and acid regurgitation for the past 12 years. Her symptoms are currently well-controlled with daily omeprazole 20 mg and adherence to lifestyle modifications, including elevating the head of her bed and avoiding late-night meals. She has no difficulty swallowing, painful swallowing, or unintended weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and obesity. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke half a pack of cigarettes daily. Her family history is notable for a brother who was diagnosed with esophageal adenocarcinoma at age 55. On physical examination, her BMI is 33 kg/m². Abdominal examination is unremarkable, and a fecal occult blood test is negative.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Reassurance and continuation of current management
answer_choices.C: Esophageal manometry
answer_choices.D: 24-hour ambulatory esophageal pH monitoring
answer_choices.E: Increase omeprazole to twice daily

explanation: The patient meets the criteria for screening for Barrett esophagus (BE) despite being female and under the age of 50. According to the American College of Gastroenterology (ACG) guidelines, screening for BE with an upper endoscopy (EGD) is recommended in men with chronic (>5 years) and/or frequent (weekly) symptoms of GERD who have two or more risk factors. While women have a significantly lower risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma, screening is recommended in women with chronic GERD if they have multiple risk factors. These risk factors include: age >50 years, Caucasian race, central obesity (BMI >30 or increased waist circumference), current or past history of smoking, and a first-degree relative with BE or esophageal adenocarcinoma. This patient has four risk factors: Caucasian race, obesity (BMI 33), smoking history, and a positive family history (brother with adenocarcinoma). Even in the absence of 'alarm symptoms' (dysphagia, weight loss, anemia), her high-risk profile warrants a one-time screening EGD.

distractor_explanations.A: Reassurance is inappropriate because the patient has a high-risk profile for Barrett esophagus. While her symptoms are controlled, screening for intestinal metaplasia is indicated to prevent or detect early progression to adenocarcinoma.
distractor_explanations.C: Esophageal manometry is used to evaluate esophageal motility disorders (e.g., achalasia) or as part of a preoperative workup for anti-reflux surgery. It is not used to screen for Barrett esophagus.
distractor_explanations.D: 24-hour pH monitoring is the gold standard for diagnosing GERD in patients with atypical symptoms or those who do not respond to a PPI trial. This patient has a confirmed clinical diagnosis that is well-controlled; pH monitoring does not evaluate for the structural changes of Barrett esophagus.
distractor_explanations.E: Increasing the PPI dose is not indicated because her symptoms are already well-controlled on a standard daily dose. Furthermore, increasing the dose does not address the need for Barrett esophagus screening.

learning_objective: Recognize the criteria for Barrett esophagus screening in patients with chronic GERD, particularly in high-risk women with multiple risk factors such as smoking, obesity, Caucasian race, and positive family history.
key_concept: Screening for Barrett esophagus is indicated in patients with chronic GERD (>5 years) and multiple risk factors (Male sex, age >50, Caucasian, smoking, obesity, family history), even in the absence of alarm symptoms.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the community health center by his mother for a three-month history of a persistent dry cough and recurring epigastric discomfort. The symptoms primarily occur at night and are often accompanied by a sour taste in the back of his throat. His mother notes that the symptoms are most frequent after he consumes a large dinner or a late-evening snack. He has no history of asthma, atopy, or weight loss. His BMI is at the 92nd percentile for his age. Physical examination is unremarkable, with no wheezing or abdominal tenderness noted. For the past four weeks, the mother has excluded spicy foods and carbonated beverages from his diet, but the symptoms persist four to five nights per week.
question_stem: Which of the following is the next best step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Initiation of a 4- to 8-week trial of a daily proton pump inhibitor
answer_choices.B: Upper gastrointestinal series to evaluate for anatomical abnormalities
answer_choices.C: Referral for 24-hour esophageal pH impedance monitoring
answer_choices.D: Formal pulmonary function testing with a methacholine challenge
answer_choices.E: Addition of a bedtime H2-receptor antagonist to current lifestyle changes

explanation: This patient presents with classic symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), including heartburn (epigastric discomfort), regurgitation (sour taste), and extra-esophageal symptoms (nocturnal cough). In children and adolescents with typical symptoms of GERD that have not responded to initial lifestyle modifications (such as dietary triggers and avoiding late-night meals), the next best step is an empiric trial of acid-suppression therapy. A 4- to 8-week trial of a daily proton pump inhibitor (PPI) is the first-line medical treatment for persistent GERD symptoms and can also serve as a diagnostic tool. In this case, the patient's obesity (BMI at the 92nd percentile) is a contributing risk factor.

distractor_explanations.A: PPIs are the most effective medical therapy for GERD and are indicated when lifestyle modifications fail.
distractor_explanations.B: An upper GI series (barium swallow) is primarily used to detect structural abnormalities like malrotation or strictures but is not sensitive for diagnosing GERD or managing its typical symptoms.
distractor_explanations.C: 24-hour pH monitoring is generally reserved for patients with refractory symptoms, cases where the diagnosis is unclear, or to correlate symptoms with reflux episodes; it is not the initial next step after failing lifestyle changes.
distractor_explanations.D: While nocturnal cough can be a sign of asthma, the presence of epigastric pain and a sour taste makes GERD more likely. Methacholine challenge is used to diagnose asthma when PFTs are inconclusive but is not the first priority here.
distractor_explanations.E: H2-receptor antagonists can be used for mild, intermittent symptoms, but for persistent symptoms (4-5 nights per week) and a trial meant to evaluate treatment response, PPIs are superior and preferred as the first-line medical trial.

learning_objective: Recognize that children with persistent GERD symptoms failing lifestyle modifications should receive a 4- to 8-week trial of a proton pump inhibitor.
key_concept: Pediatric GERD management involves lifestyle changes followed by an empiric trial of PPI therapy for persistent symptoms.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a return of solid-food dysphagia and persistent heartburn. She has a 10-year history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) managed with omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. Three months ago, she underwent an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) that revealed a 4-cm sliding hiatal hernia and a distal esophageal stricture. The stricture was dilated at that time, and biopsies were negative for Barrett's esophagus or malignancy. However, her symptoms recurred shortly after the procedure. She is frustrated with her symptoms and the requirement for high-dose medications and wishes to pursue definitive surgical management. Her BMI is 31 kg/m².
question_stem: Which of the following is the most essential next step in the preoperative evaluation of this patient before proceeding with a Nissen fundoplication?

answer_choices.A: 24-hour ambulatory pH monitoring
answer_choices.C: Computed tomography (CT) of the chest and abdomen
answer_choices.D: Gastric emptying study
answer_choices.E: Repeat upper endoscopy with multiple quadrant biopsies

explanation: Before performing anti-reflux surgery (such as a Nissen fundoplication), it is essential to perform esophageal manometry. The purpose of manometry is twofold: first, to rule out primary motility disorders (such as achalasia) that can mimic GERD symptoms but would be worsened by a fundoplication; and second, to assess the strength of esophageal peristalsis. If a patient has severely impaired peristalsis (ineffective esophageal motility), a partial fundoplication (e.g., Toupet/270-degree wrap) may be preferred over a total 360-degree Nissen wrap to reduce the risk of postoperative dysphagia. In this patient, the presence of a peptic stricture already provides objective evidence of GERD, making surgery a viable option for her refractory symptoms.

distractor_explanations.A: 24-hour pH monitoring is the 'gold standard' for diagnosing GERD when the diagnosis is unclear. However, in a patient with definitive endoscopic evidence of reflux-induced complications (like a peptic stricture or Grade C/D esophagitis), the diagnosis is already established, and pH monitoring is not strictly required before surgery.
distractor_explanations.C: CT imaging is not part of the standard preoperative workup for GERD or a sliding hiatal hernia. It may be useful in complex paraesophageal hernias to assess the contents of the hernia sac, but it does not provide the functional information needed for fundoplication planning.
distractor_explanations.D: A gastric emptying study is indicated only if the patient has symptoms suggestive of gastroparesis (e.g., significant bloating, postprandial emesis, early satiety). While gastroparesis can contribute to GERD, it is not a routine preoperative requirement for all fundoplication candidates.
distractor_explanations.E: The patient had an endoscopy with biopsies only three months ago which ruled out malignancy. While the stricture has recurred, repeating the biopsies so soon is not the 'most essential' next step for surgical planning compared to functional manometry.

learning_objective: Identify esophageal manometry as a required preoperative step before anti-reflux surgery to rule out motility disorders and guide the choice of surgical technique.
key_concept: Preoperative workup for anti-reflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) requires manometry to exclude achalasia and evaluate esophageal peristalsis.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 64-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-hour history of sudden-onset, excruciating epigastric pain that has now become generalized. He has a medical history of chronic osteoarthritis and has been taking high-dose ibuprofen for the past six months. On examination, he appears in significant distress, diaphoretic, and is lying perfectly still. His temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F), heart rate is 114/min, and blood pressure is 104/66 mmHg. The abdomen is rigid and "board-like" with diffuse guarding and rebound tenderness in all quadrants. An upright chest radiograph reveals a thin crescent of lucency beneath the right hemidiaphragm. After aggressive fluid resuscitation and administration of intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics, the patient is taken for an emergent laparotomy. During the procedure, a 1-cm perforation is identified on the anterior surface of the gastric antrum.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate additional step to perform during this surgical procedure?

answer_choices.A: Highly selective vagotomy
answer_choices.C: Total gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction
answer_choices.D: Splenectomy
answer_choices.E: Truncal vagotomy and pyloroplasty

explanation: This patient presents with a perforated peptic ulcer, evidenced by sudden severe pain, a rigid abdomen, and pneumoperitoneum (free air under the diaphragm) on upright chest X-ray. The perforation is located in the gastric antrum. Unlike duodenal ulcers, which are virtually never malignant, approximately 5-10% of gastric ulcers are associated with an underlying gastric adenocarcinoma. Therefore, the standard surgical management for a perforated gastric ulcer includes not only the repair of the defect (typically with an omental/Graham patch) but also a biopsy of the ulcer margin to screen for malignancy. If the patient is stable, a wedge resection of the ulcer can also be performed to provide a definitive diagnosis and manage the perforation simultaneously.

distractor_explanations.A: Highly selective vagotomy is a procedure designed to reduce acid secretion for refractory duodenal ulcers. It is not indicated in the acute management of a perforated gastric ulcer and does not address the risk of malignancy.
distractor_explanations.C: Total gastrectomy is an overly aggressive and morbid procedure for a primary perforation of a 1-cm ulcer unless malignancy has already been confirmed and requires such a radical resection.
distractor_explanations.D: Splenectomy is not indicated in the management of peptic ulcer disease unless there is collateral damage to the splenic artery or spleen during a complex surgery, or in specific cases of gastric cancer involving the splenic hilum.
distractor_explanations.E: Truncal vagotomy and pyloroplasty were historically used to treat refractory peptic ulcer disease by reducing acid production. However, they are not standard in the acute management of a perforated gastric ulcer, especially since they do not address the primary need to screen for malignancy.

learning_objective: During the surgical repair of a perforated gastric ulcer, a biopsy of the ulcer margin must be performed to screen for underlying gastric adenocarcinoma.
key_concept: Surgical management of perforated peptic ulcers: gastric ulcers require biopsy for malignancy screening, while duodenal ulcers do not.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 14-year-old female with a history of juvenile idiopathic arthritis is admitted to the inpatient ward following two episodes of melena and an episode of syncope at home. She has been taking high-dose naproxen for the past month due to a joint flare. On arrival, her blood pressure was 92/58 mmHg and pulse was 122/min; however, her vitals stabilized after a 2-L bolus of normal saline. Her hemoglobin is 7.4 g/dL (baseline 12.5 g/dL). An urgent upper endoscopy reveals a 1.5-cm ulcer on the posterior wall of the duodenal bulb. The ulcer base contains a non-bleeding visible vessel (Forrest IIa stigmata), which is successfully treated with a combination of dilute epinephrine injection and the placement of two hemoclips. Biopsies for Helicobacter pylori were taken but are currently pending. The patient is transferred back to the ward.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the pharmacological management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Initiate oral omeprazole 20 mg once daily
answer_choices.B: Initiate a continuous intravenous pantoprazole infusion
answer_choices.C: Initiate oral misoprostol 200 mcg four times daily
answer_choices.D: Administer oral sucralfate suspension every 6 hours
answer_choices.E: Initiate intravenous famotidine 20 mg twice daily

explanation: This patient has a high-risk peptic ulcer (Forrest IIa: non-bleeding visible vessel) located in the posterior duodenum. Posterior duodenal ulcers are particularly dangerous because of their proximity to the gastroduodenal artery, which can lead to life-threatening hemorrhage. Following successful endoscopic hemostasis of high-risk stigmata (active bleeding, a visible vessel, or an adherent clot), the standard of care is high-dose proton pump inhibitor (PPI) therapy for 72 hours. The goal is to maintain the gastric pH above 6.0, which prevents the acid-induced degradation of fibrin clots and optimizes platelet aggregation. While high-dose oral PPIs are increasingly used, the classic board-tested management for high-risk lesions post-endoscopy is a continuous intravenous PPI infusion (e.g., pantoprazole) for 72 hours.

distractor_explanations.A: Standard-dose oral PPI (20 mg daily) is appropriate for maintenance therapy or for low-risk ulcers (e.g., clean-based ulcers, Forrest III), but it is insufficient for the acute management of a high-risk visible vessel which carries a high risk of rebleeding.
distractor_explanations.C: Misoprostol is a prostaglandin E1 analog used for the prevention of NSAID-induced ulcers. It is not used for the treatment of acute hemorrhage. Furthermore, it is generally avoided in females of childbearing age due to its abortifacient properties.
distractor_explanations.D: Sucralfate provides a physical barrier over the ulcer base but does not significantly raise the gastric pH. It is not indicated for the management of acute gastrointestinal bleeding or for the stabilization of clots after endoscopic therapy.
distractor_explanations.E: H2-receptor antagonists like famotidine are significantly less effective than PPIs at maintaining the gastric pH above 6.0 and have no role in the acute management of high-risk bleeding peptic ulcers.

learning_objective: Following endoscopic hemostasis of a high-risk peptic ulcer (active bleeding or visible vessel), patients should receive high-dose intravenous PPI therapy for 72 hours to reduce the risk of rebleeding.
key_concept: Management of high-risk peptic ulcer bleeding (Forrest Class I/IIa) with high-dose IV PPIs post-endoscopy.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 22-year-old male athlete presents to the outpatient clinic reporting several weeks of progressive fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance. He denies any epigastric pain, nausea, or changes in bowel habits. Physical examination is notable for conjunctival pallor but no abdominal tenderness. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin of 9.4 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume of 72 fL, and a low ferritin level. A fecal occult blood test is positive. Subsequent upper endoscopy reveals a 1.5-cm ulcer in the duodenal bulb; a biopsy-based rapid urease test is positive for Helicobacter pylori. The patient is prescribed a 14-day course of omeprazole, clarithromycin, and amoxicillin. He completes the antibiotic course but continues to take the daily omeprazole for symptom prevention. It has now been four weeks since he finished the antibiotics. He returns for a follow-up to ensure the infection has been cleared.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm eradication of the infection?

answer_choices.A: Perform H. pylori serology today
answer_choices.B: Perform a urea breath test today
answer_choices.C: Discontinue omeprazole and perform a urea breath test in 2 weeks
answer_choices.D: Repeat the upper endoscopy with biopsy today
answer_choices.E: Discontinue omeprazole and perform a stool antigen test in 2 days

explanation: To ensure patient safety and diagnostic accuracy, Helicobacter pylori eradication must be confirmed in all patients treated for Peptic Ulcer Disease (PUD). The most appropriate non-invasive tests for confirming eradication are the urea breath test or the stool antigen test. However, these tests are highly sensitive to medications that reduce the bacterial load or urease activity without fully eradicating the organism. To avoid false-negative results, testing must be delayed until at least 4 weeks after the completion of antibiotic therapy and at least 2 weeks after the discontinuation of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs). Since this patient finished his antibiotics 4 weeks ago but is still taking omeprazole, he must discontinue the PPI and wait 2 weeks before testing.

distractor_explanations.A: Serology (IgG antibodies) remains positive for months or years after the infection has been cleared. It is useful for initial diagnosis in treatment-naive patients but cannot be used to confirm eradication.
distractor_explanations.B: Performing a urea breath test while the patient is still taking a PPI increases the risk of a false-negative result because PPIs have minor anti-H. pylori activity and inhibit the urease enzyme used for detection.
distractor_explanations.D: Repeat upper endoscopy is generally not required for duodenal ulcers to confirm healing or rule out malignancy, as duodenal ulcers are rarely malignant. It is also more invasive and expensive than breath or stool testing for confirming H. pylori eradication.
distractor_explanations.E: The stool antigen test, like the urea breath test, requires a 2-week washout period for PPIs and a 4-week washout for antibiotics. Testing after only 2 days of stopping the PPI would likely result in a false negative.

learning_objective: Accurate H. pylori eradication testing requires a washout period of at least 4 weeks for antibiotics and 2 weeks for proton pump inhibitors.
key_concept: H. pylori eradication confirmation and PPI interference.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 22-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 1-month history of burning epigastric pain. She reports that the pain is often most severe late at night and is frequently relieved for several hours after eating a meal. She denies any nausea, vomiting, or changes in her bowel habits. She has no history of weight loss, dysphagia, or dark, tarry stools. She takes no regular medications, including over-the-counter pain relievers, and does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg, and heart rate is 74/min. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness to deep palpation in the epigastrium without guarding, rebound, or palpable masses. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 13.8 g/dL and a normal white blood cell count.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Serum Helicobacter pylori immunoglobulin G antibody testing
answer_choices.C: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy
answer_choices.D: Abdominal ultrasonography
answer_choices.E: Empiric treatment with a proton pump inhibitor, amoxicillin, and clarithromycin

explanation: The patient presents with classic symptoms of a duodenal ulcer (burning epigastric pain relieved by food) and is under the age of 60 with no 'alarm symptoms' (e.g., weight loss, anemia, dysphagia, persistent vomiting, or occult GI bleeding). According to current clinical guidelines (such as those from the American College of Gastroenterology), the recommended management for patients < 60 years old with dyspepsia and no alarm symptoms is a 'test-and-treat' strategy for Helicobacter pylori using non-invasive testing. The urea breath test and stool antigen test are the preferred non-invasive tests because they identify active infection. If positive, eradication therapy is initiated.

distractor_explanations.A: Serum H. pylori IgG testing (serology) cannot differentiate between a past, resolved infection and a current, active infection. Therefore, it is no longer recommended as a primary diagnostic tool for active dyspepsia management.
distractor_explanations.C: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is the gold standard for diagnosing peptic ulcer disease and ruling out malignancy. However, it is reserved as the initial step for patients ≥ 60 years old or those of any age with 'alarm symptoms' (e.g., weight loss, anemia, dysphagia). In a young, stable patient without alarm features, non-invasive testing is the more cost-effective first step.
distractor_explanations.D: Abdominal ultrasonography is primarily used to evaluate for biliary pathology (e.g., cholelithiasis or cholecystitis), which often presents with right upper quadrant pain and nausea, usually triggered by meals rather than relieved by them.
distractor_explanations.E: Triple therapy (PPI and two antibiotics) should only be initiated after H. pylori infection has been confirmed by testing. Starting antibiotics empirically contributes to antibiotic resistance and is not recommended before a positive test result.

learning_objective: In patients younger than 60 years with dyspepsia and no alarm symptoms, the most appropriate next step is non-invasive testing for H. pylori (urea breath test or stool antigen) rather than endoscopy.
key_concept: H. pylori 'Test-and-Treat' strategy
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 67-year-old female presents to an urgent care center with a 1-week history of dysuria and the sensation of passing air in her urine. She reports that her urine has become increasingly cloudy and foul-smelling. For the past 6 months, she has experienced intermittent, crampy right lower quadrant (RLQ) abdominal pain, 3 to 5 loose, non-bloody stools daily, and a 14-lb weight loss. She has a 40-pack-year smoking history and a medical history significant for recurrent calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis and a cholecystectomy for gallstones. Her temperature is 101.1°F (38.4°C), heart rate 94/min, and blood pressure 132/78 mmHg. Abdominal examination reveals mild tenderness in the RLQ without guarding or rebound. A prior urine culture from her primary care physician 10 days ago showed a polymicrobial growth of Escherichia coli, Enterococcus faecalis, and Klebsiella pneumoniae.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Cystoscopy
answer_choices.C: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with oral contrast
answer_choices.D: Flexible sigmoidoscopy
answer_choices.E: Small bowel follow-through with barium

explanation: This patient's presentation—pneumaturia (air in the urine), fecaluria, and recurrent polymicrobial UTIs—is pathognomonic for an enterovesical fistula. Given her 6-month history of RLQ pain, non-bloody diarrhea, smoking history, and weight loss, Crohn disease (CD) is the most likely underlying cause. Transmural inflammation is a hallmark of CD, which can lead to the formation of fistulas between the bowel and other organs (e.g., bladder, skin, or other bowel loops). The history of calcium oxalate stones and gallstones are classic extraintestinal associations with terminal ileal CD (due to fat and bile acid malabsorption). The next best step to confirm the presence of an enterovesical fistula and evaluate the extent of bowel involvement is a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with oral or rectal contrast. Contrast appearing in the bladder that did not arrive via the kidneys (since it was given orally/rectally) confirms the fistula.

distractor_explanations.A: Cystoscopy can identify an area of inflammation or a 'cone sign' at the site of a fistula, but its sensitivity for detecting the fistula tract itself is lower than CT (approximately 35-45%). It is often used to rule out primary bladder malignancy but is not the preferred initial test.
distractor_explanations.B: While colonoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosing the underlying Crohn disease (showing skip lesions and cobblestoning), it is not the most appropriate immediate step for a patient with a suspected acute complication like an enterovesical fistula. Imaging is required first to characterize the fistula and check for associated abscesses.
distractor_explanations.D: Flexible sigmoidoscopy only evaluates the distal colon. Crohn disease most frequently involves the terminal ileum and cecum (RLQ), which would be missed. Furthermore, it does not evaluate for the presence of a fistula tract.
distractor_explanations.E: Small bowel follow-through can identify a 'string sign' in CD, but it is less sensitive than CT for characterizing extraluminal complications like fistulas or abscesses and is largely superseded by CT or MR enterography.

learning_objective: Recognize enterovesical fistula as a transmural complication of Crohn disease and identify CT scan with oral or rectal contrast as the preferred diagnostic step.
key_concept: Enterovesical fistula in Crohn disease presents with pneumaturia and polymicrobial UTIs; CT with contrast is the initial diagnostic test of choice.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 1-week history of pneumaturia (passage of air in the urine) and recurrent, foul-smelling urinary tract infections. She has a 25-year history of Crohn's disease that has primarily involved her terminal ileum. Vital signs include a temperature of 37.9°C (100.2°F), heart rate of 88/min, and blood pressure of 132/80 mmHg. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant of the abdomen without rebound or guarding. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 13,000/mm³ and an elevated C-reactive protein. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with oral contrast demonstrates a fistulous tract between the terminal ileum and the bladder dome, with air and oral contrast visible within the urinary bladder.
question_stem: Which of the following pharmacological agents is most appropriate to promote closure of this patient's fistula?

answer_choices.A: Sulfasalazine
answer_choices.C: Infliximab
answer_choices.D: Metronidazole
answer_choices.E: Azathioprine

explanation: The patient has Crohn's disease (CD) complicated by an enterovesical fistula, as evidenced by pneumaturia, recurrent UTIs, and imaging findings. Fistulas are a hallmark of CD due to its transmural nature (inflammation extending through all layers of the bowel wall). Anti-tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-alpha agents, such as infliximab or adalimumab, are the first-line medical therapy for inducing and maintaining remission in fistulizing Crohn's disease. They are specifically indicated to promote the closure of perianal and internal fistulas.

distractor_explanations.A: Sulfasalazine and other 5-aminosalicylates (5-ASAs) are primarily used for the treatment of mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis or colonic Crohn's disease. They have no efficacy in treating transmural complications like fistulas.
distractor_explanations.B: Budesonide is a controlled-release corticosteroid with high first-pass metabolism used for the induction of remission in mild-to-moderate Crohn's disease localized to the terminal ileum or right colon. It does not treat fistulizing disease.
distractor_explanations.C: This is the correct choice as Anti-TNF agents are the gold standard for medical management of fistulizing CD.
distractor_explanations.D: Metronidazole and ciprofloxacin are often used as adjunctive treatments for perianal fistulas or to treat the associated urinary tract infection, but they are generally not effective as monotherapy for the long-term closure of internal (e.g., enterovesical) fistulas.
distractor_explanations.E: Azathioprine is a thiopurine used for the maintenance of remission in Crohn's disease. While it can be used to maintain fistula closure, it has a slow onset of action (3-6 months) and is not the appropriate first-line agent for acute induction of fistula closure.

learning_objective: Recognize that anti-TNF agents (e.g., infliximab) are the first-line pharmacological treatment for inducing closure of fistulas in patients with Crohn's disease.
key_concept: Fistulizing Crohn's disease management (Anti-TNF therapy).
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for a 2-day history of worsening right lower quadrant abdominal pain and a low-grade fever. His parents report that he has had intermittent abdominal cramping and 3 to 4 loose, non-bloody stools daily for the past 6 months. During this time, he has lost 4 kg (8.8 lb). A review of his growth chart reveals that his height has dropped from the 50th percentile to the 10th percentile over the last year. Physical examination shows three small, shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and a non-tender, flesh-colored skin tag in the perianal area. There is tenderness to palpation in the right lower quadrant without guarding or rebound. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 11,500/mm³, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/h.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis, and which screening measure is required before beginning the preferred biological therapy for this patient's condition?

answer_choices.A: Crohn disease; interferon-gamma release assay
answer_choices.B: Ulcerative colitis; p-ANCA and ASCA serology
answer_choices.C: Celiac disease; annual bone mineral density monitoring
answer_choices.D: Crohn disease; colonoscopy starting 1 year after diagnosis
answer_choices.E: Ulcerative colitis; annual screening for primary sclerosing cholangitis

explanation: The patient's clinical presentation is highly suggestive of Crohn disease (CD). Key features include chronic non-bloody diarrhea, weight loss, growth failure (height percentile drop), right lower quadrant pain (indicating terminal ileitis), aphthous ulcers, and perianal disease (skin tags). Unlike ulcerative colitis (UC), which is limited to the mucosa and involves the rectum, CD is transmural, can affect any part of the GI tract, and frequently involves the perianal region. In pediatric patients with moderate-to-severe CD, biological therapy with anti-TNF-α agents (e.g., infliximab, adalimumab) is often used early ('top-down' approach) to induce remission and promote growth. Before starting anti-TNF-α therapy, screening for latent tuberculosis with an interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) or a tuberculin skin test (PPD) is mandatory to prevent reactivation. Screening for Hepatitis B is also typically performed.

distractor_explanations.A: This is the correct diagnosis and the required pre-biological screening step.
distractor_explanations.B: Ulcerative colitis typically presents with bloody diarrhea and continuous colonic involvement starting from the rectum. p-ANCA and ASCA are serological markers that can aid in diagnosis (ASCA is more common in CD, p-ANCA in UC), but they are not 'screening measures' required before therapy.
distractor_explanations.C: Celiac disease can cause growth failure and abdominal pain, but it does not cause perianal skin tags or oral ulcers. While patients with Celiac disease may need bone density monitoring due to malabsorption, it is not the most likely diagnosis here.
distractor_explanations.D: Crohn disease is the correct diagnosis, but surveillance colonoscopy for colorectal cancer (CRC) is generally recommended starting 8–10 years after the onset of symptoms for patients with colonic involvement, not 1 year after diagnosis.
distractor_explanations.E: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is more strongly associated with ulcerative colitis than Crohn disease. While monitoring liver function tests is important in IBD, routine annual screening for PSC (e.g., with imaging) in the absence of elevated liver enzymes is not standard practice.

learning_objective: Recognize the clinical presentation of pediatric Crohn disease and identify the necessity of screening for latent tuberculosis with an interferon-gamma release assay or PPD prior to initiating anti-TNF-α therapy.
key_concept: Pediatric Crohn disease often presents with growth failure and perianal disease; anti-TNF-α therapy requires pre-treatment screening for latent tuberculosis and Hepatitis B.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 28-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of sudden, severe, colicky right-sided flank pain that radiates to her groin. She also reports nausea and one episode of non-bilious vomiting. Her medical history is significant for Crohn disease involving the terminal ileum, diagnosed eight years ago. She has undergone two prior surgical resections of the distal ileum due to recurrent strictures and chronic obstruction. Her current medications include adalimumab and a daily multivitamin. On physical examination, the patient is afebrile and appears in distress, frequently shifting positions on the gurney. There is significant tenderness at the right costovertebral angle. A urinalysis reveals 3+ blood, no protein, and no nitrites or leukocyte esterase.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most likely mechanism for this patient’s acute presentation?

answer_choices.A: Increased biliary cholesterol concentration due to decreased bile acid recycling
answer_choices.B: Increased intestinal absorption of oxalate due to fat malabsorption
answer_choices.C: Reduced renal perfusion from chronic volume depletion and diarrhea
answer_choices.D: Formation of a fistulous tract between the ileum and the bladder
answer_choices.E: Deposition of insoluble protein fibrils in the renal parenchyma

explanation: This patient is presenting with acute nephrolithiasis (renal colic), characterized by sudden-onset flank pain radiating to the groin and hematuria. In patients with Crohn disease, especially those with terminal ileal involvement or resection, there is a significantly increased risk of calcium oxalate kidney stones. Normally, dietary calcium binds to oxalate in the gut lumen, forming an insoluble complex that is excreted in the feces. However, in Crohn disease with ileal dysfunction, bile acids are not properly reabsorbed. This leads to fat malabsorption. The unabsorbed fatty acids in the intestinal lumen bind to calcium (saponification), leaving oxalate free. This 'free' oxalate is then excessively absorbed in the colon (enteric hyperoxaluria) and subsequently excreted by the kidneys, where it binds to urinary calcium to form stones.

distractor_explanations.A: This describes the mechanism for cholesterol gallstones (cholelithiasis). While Crohn disease patients with ileal resection are at increased risk for gallstones due to the loss of bile acids, gallstones typically present with right upper quadrant pain and would not cause hematuria or flank pain radiating to the groin.
distractor_explanations.C: Chronic dehydration from diarrhea can lead to concentrated urine and potentially uric acid stones, but the specific, high-yield association with ileal Crohn disease and fat malabsorption is enteric hyperoxaluria leading to calcium oxalate stones.
distractor_explanations.D: Enterovesical fistulas are a known complication of Crohn disease, but they typically present with pneumaturia (air in the urine), fecaluria, and recurrent urinary tract infections rather than acute colicky flank pain and hematuria.
distractor_explanations.E: This describes AA amyloidosis, a potential complication of chronic inflammatory conditions like Crohn disease. However, renal amyloidosis typically presents with nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria and edema), not acute renal colic.

learning_objective: Understand the pathophysiology of calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis in patients with Crohn disease involving the terminal ileum.
key_concept: Fat malabsorption in Crohn disease leads to enteric hyperoxaluria because malabsorbed fats bind calcium, leaving oxalate free to be absorbed in the colon.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 67-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of worsening crampy abdominal pain, distension, and several episodes of bilious vomiting. He has a 38-year history of Crohn disease, characterized by transmural inflammation and skip lesions involving the terminal ileum and the majority of the large bowel, sparing only the rectum. His home medications include adalimumab and azathioprine, though his adherence has been intermittent. On physical examination, he is tachycardic but afebrile. His abdomen is markedly distended and tympanic, with high-pitched "tinkling" bowel sounds. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals multiple dilated loops of small bowel with a transition point at a known stricture in the distal ileum. Laboratory evaluation shows an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 58 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein of 42 mg/L. After stabilization with nasogastric decompression, the patient asks about his long-term risk of colon cancer, noting he has not had a colonoscopy in nearly 12 years.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate screening recommendation for this patient, and what is the underlying molecular pathophysiology justifying this strategy?

answer_choices.A: Colonoscopy every 10 years; the transmural nature of Crohn disease prevents the mucosal dysplasia-carcinoma sequence.
answer_choices.B: Colonoscopy every 1–3 years; chronic Th1/Th17-mediated inflammation drives an early p53 mutation and late APC loss sequence.
answer_choices.C: Colonoscopy every 5 years; the presence of skip lesions reduces the field effect of carcinogenesis compared to continuous colitis.
answer_choices.D: Annual fecal immunochemical testing (FIT); Crohn-associated malignancy primarily presents as extraluminal masses that are poorly visualized on endoscopy.
answer_choices.E: No screening required for the colon; the risk of adenocarcinoma in Crohn disease is confined to the small bowel regardless of colonic involvement.

explanation: Patients with Crohn disease involving at least one-third of the colon carry a risk of colorectal cancer (CRC) similar to those with ulcerative colitis. The standard screening recommendation for these patients is surveillance colonoscopy every 1–3 years, beginning 8 years after the onset of symptoms. The molecular pathophysiology of IBD-associated CRC differs from the sporadic 'Vogelstein' adenoma-carcinoma sequence. In sporadic CRC, the 'gatekeeper' mutation is usually the loss of the APC gene (early), followed by KRAS mutations and finally p53 loss (late). In contrast, chronic inflammation in Crohn disease (primarily mediated by Th1 and Th17 cytokines like TNF-α, IFN-γ, and IL-17) leads to an 'inflammation-dysplasia-carcinoma' sequence. In this pathway, p53 mutations often occur very early—frequently in histologically non-dysplastic but chronically inflamed mucosa—while APC mutations occur later in the process. This 'field effect' of early p53 mutations necessitates frequent endoscopic surveillance with biopsies to detect flat, non-polypoid dysplasia.
