distractor_explanations.A: A 10-year interval is for the general population starting at age 45. The transmural nature of Crohn disease does not prevent mucosal dysplasia; in fact, it is associated with a high risk of malignancy when the colon is involved.
distractor_explanations.C: A 5-year interval is not standard for any IBD surveillance protocol. Skip lesions do not reduce the risk of cancer; the risk is determined by the total duration and cumulative burden of inflammation.
distractor_explanations.D: FIT is not an appropriate surveillance tool for patients with IBD, as chronic inflammation and mucosal friability often lead to false positives (blood in stool without malignancy) and false negatives (missing flat dysplasia). IBD-associated CRC typically arises from the mucosa, not as extraluminal masses.
distractor_explanations.E: This is incorrect. While Crohn disease does increase the risk of small bowel adenocarcinoma (which is rare), colonic involvement carries a significant risk of CRC, similar to ulcerative colitis.

learning_objective: Recognize that Crohn disease with significant colonic involvement requires colorectal cancer surveillance every 1–3 years starting 8 years after diagnosis, driven by a molecular sequence featuring early p53 mutations.
key_concept: IBD-associated colorectal cancer screening and molecular pathophysiology (p53-early vs. APC-early).
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic for a routine follow-up regarding his ulcerative colitis. He was diagnosed with pancolitis at the age of 24 after presenting with three months of bloody diarrhea and abdominal cramping. His disease is currently well-controlled on oral mesalamine and occasional mesalamine suppositories; he reports no flares in the last three years. He has no family history of colorectal cancer. His last colonoscopy, performed at the time of diagnosis, showed continuous mucosal inflammation from the rectum to the cecum with crypt abscesses. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals a non-tender abdomen and no skin lesions or joint swelling. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 14.2 g/dL and a normal C-reactive protein level.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the health maintenance of this patient’s gastrointestinal condition?

answer_choices.A: Colonoscopy starting at age 45 and every 10 years thereafter
answer_choices.B: Screening colonoscopy now and every 1-2 years thereafter
answer_choices.C: Annual fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) starting now
answer_choices.D: Colonoscopy in 6 years (10 years before the standard screening age)
answer_choices.E: Prophylactic total proctocolectomy

explanation: Patients with ulcerative colitis (UC) have a significantly increased risk of developing colorectal cancer (CRC). The risk is correlated with the duration of the disease (typically increasing after 8-10 years) and the extent of colonic involvement (pancolitis > left-sided colitis). According to current guidelines, surveillance colonoscopy with regular biopsies should begin 8 years after the initial diagnosis for patients with pancolitis (or 12 years for those with left-sided colitis). Since this patient was diagnosed 10 years ago and has pancolitis, he is now due for his first surveillance colonoscopy. Following the initial screening, colonoscopies should be repeated every 1-2 years to monitor for dysplasia.

distractor_explanations.A: This represents the standard CRC screening guideline for the general population at average risk, which begins at age 45. Patients with UC require much earlier and more frequent surveillance due to their high-risk status.
distractor_explanations.C: Fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) or fecal occult blood testing (FOBT) is used for average-risk CRC screening. It is not sufficient for surveillance in patients with inflammatory bowel disease, as these patients require direct visualization and biopsies to detect flat dysplasia.
distractor_explanations.D: This might be confused with screening for individuals with a family history of CRC (starting at age 40 or 10 years before the relative's diagnosis). UC screening is based on the time elapsed since the IBD diagnosis, not the patient's absolute age.
distractor_explanations.E: Total proctocolectomy is curative for UC and eliminates the risk of CRC, but it is not indicated for primary prevention in a patient whose disease is well-controlled. It is reserved for patients with high-grade dysplasia, multi-focal low-grade dysplasia, or medically refractory disease.

learning_objective: Identify the appropriate timing and frequency for colorectal cancer surveillance in patients with long-standing ulcerative colitis.
key_concept: Colorectal cancer screening in ulcerative colitis begins 8-10 years after diagnosis and occurs every 1-2 years thereafter.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old male with a 32-year history of ulcerative colitis presents to the emergency department with four days of worsening bloody diarrhea, tenesmus, and severe abdominal pain. He reports over 10 bowel movements daily and feels increasingly lightheaded. His symptoms have worsened since he quit smoking six months ago. On examination, he appears toxic and lethargic. Vital signs are: temperature 39.4°C (103°F), blood pressure 90/58 mmHg, heart rate 134/min, and respiratory rate 24/min. The abdomen is markedly distended and tympanic to percussion, with diffuse tenderness and guarding. Laboratory evaluation reveals a leukocyte count of 24,000/mm³ and a hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL. An urgent plain abdominal radiograph demonstrates a dilated transverse colon measuring 8.5 cm with a lack of haustral markings and no evidence of free intraperitoneal air.
question_stem: In addition to aggressive fluid resuscitation and nasogastric tube decompression, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_choices.A: Perform urgent flexible sigmoidoscopy to assess for pseudomembranes
answer_choices.B: Initiate intravenous corticosteroids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and surgical consultation
answer_choices.C: Administer a single dose of infliximab as rescue therapy
answer_choices.D: Proceed to immediate total proctocolectomy with end ileostomy
answer_choices.E: Perform a barium enema to evaluate for a distal colonic obstruction

explanation: This patient presents with toxic megacolon (TM), a life-threatening complication of ulcerative colitis (UC). TM is characterized by total or segmental non-obstructive colonic dilation (>6 cm) associated with systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, and hypotension). The hallmark radiographic finding is a dilated colon, often the transverse colon, with a 'lead pipe' appearance (loss of haustral markings). Initial management involves bowel rest (NPO/NG tube), aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation, intravenous corticosteroids (to treat the underlying inflammatory flare), and broad-spectrum antibiotics (to reduce the risk of colonic perforation and treat bacterial translocation). Early surgical consultation is mandatory, as roughly 50% of patients may require emergency surgery if they do not improve within 24–72 hours of medical management.

distractor_explanations.A: Sigmoidoscopy and colonoscopy are contraindicated in patients with suspected or confirmed toxic megacolon due to the high risk of colonic perforation from air insufflation.
distractor_explanations.C: Infliximab or cyclosporine may be used as rescue therapy in patients with steroid-refractory UC, but they are not the initial treatment of choice for toxic megacolon, where IV steroids and antibiotics are the standard.
distractor_explanations.D: Immediate surgery is indicated for overt colonic perforation (free air on imaging), massive hemorrhage, or clinical deterioration despite medical management. In the absence of perforation, medical management is typically trialed for 24-72 hours.
distractor_explanations.E: Barium enema is contraindicated in the acute setting of severe UC or toxic megacolon because it can worsen the dilation and significantly increase the risk of perforation.

learning_objective: Recognize the clinical presentation of toxic megacolon and initiate appropriate medical management, including IV fluids, steroids, antibiotics, and surgical consultation, while avoiding contraindicated endoscopic or contrast studies.
key_concept: Toxic megacolon management involves medical stabilization and avoidance of colonoscopy to prevent perforation.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 67-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of increased frequency of bloody stools, tenesmus, and left-sided abdominal cramping. He was diagnosed with ulcerative pancolitis 18 years ago and has been maintained on oral mesalamine; however, he admits to being non-compliant with his gastrointestinal follow-up appointments for the past several years. On physical examination, he is afebrile with mild left lower quadrant tenderness but no signs of peritonitis. Laboratory evaluation shows a hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 34 mm/hr. After initiating treatment for his current flare, the physician discusses the patient's long-term health maintenance and the increased risk of malignancy associated with his condition.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate screening recommendation for colorectal cancer in this patient?

answer_choices.A: Colonoscopy every 10 years starting at age 45
answer_choices.B: Surveillance colonoscopy with biopsies every 1–2 years
answer_choices.C: Annual fecal immunochemical testing (FIT)
answer_choices.D: Colonoscopy every 5 years starting now
answer_choices.E: Total proctocolectomy to eliminate the risk of malignancy

explanation: Patients with ulcerative colitis (UC) have a significantly increased risk of developing colorectal cancer (CRC), particularly those with pancolitis or disease lasting longer than 8–10 years. Current guidelines recommend that patients with UC (excluding those with disease limited to the rectum) begin surveillance colonoscopy 8–10 years after the initial diagnosis. Following the initial surveillance colonoscopy, repeat colonoscopies with multiple random biopsies (or chromoendoscopy-guided biopsies) should be performed every 1–2 years to monitor for dysplasia. This patient was diagnosed 18 years ago, placing him well within the high-risk window requiring frequent surveillance.

distractor_explanations.A: This describes the standard screening recommendation for the general population at average risk for colorectal cancer (starting at age 45 and repeating every 10 years). Patients with long-standing inflammatory bowel disease require much more frequent monitoring.
distractor_explanations.C: Annual fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) is an acceptable screening method for average-risk individuals, but it is insufficient for patients with ulcerative colitis, as they require direct mucosal visualization and biopsies to detect dysplasia.
distractor_explanations.D: A 5-year interval is used for certain patients with a family history of CRC or specific types of polyps, but it is not frequent enough for the high-risk inflammatory environment of long-standing ulcerative colitis.
distractor_explanations.E: While total proctocolectomy is curative for ulcerative colitis and eliminates the risk of CRC, it is not a first-line screening or prevention strategy. Surgery is reserved for patients with medically refractory disease, toxic megacolon, or detected high-grade dysplasia/carcinoma.

learning_objective: Recognize that patients with ulcerative colitis require surveillance colonoscopy every 1–2 years starting 8–10 years after diagnosis due to an increased risk of colorectal cancer.
key_concept: Colorectal cancer surveillance in long-standing Ulcerative Colitis.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old male with a 20-year history of ulcerative colitis presents to an urgent care center with a 3-day history of worsening bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal pain, and fever. He reports having over 10 bowel movements per day and feeling increasingly lightheaded. On physical examination, he appears acutely ill. Temperature is 39.0°C (102.2°F), heart rate is 118/min, and blood pressure is 95/60 mmHg. His abdomen is severely distended and exquisitely tender to light palpation with guarding. An abdominal radiograph reveals a dilated transverse colon with a diameter of 7.5 cm and a loss of haustral markings. He is immediately transferred to the hospital, where he is started on aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and high-dose intravenous corticosteroids. Despite 24 hours of optimal medical management, his clinical condition deteriorates with worsening tachycardia, and a surgical consultation for a total proctocolectomy is obtained.
question_stem: Which of the following best describes the pathophysiological basis for why this surgical procedure is considered curative for this patient's underlying condition?

answer_choices.A: The inflammation in this disease is transmural, allowing for complete resection of affected full-thickness segments.
answer_choices.B: The inflammatory process is confined to the mucosal and submucosal layers of the colon and rectum.
answer_choices.C: The surgery removes the primary site of p-ANCA production, which is the driver of systemic inflammation.
answer_choices.D: The disease is characterized by skip lesions that are permanently eradicated by the removal of the terminal ileum.
answer_choices.E: Total proctocolectomy leads to the resolution of all extraintestinal manifestations, including primary sclerosing cholangitis.

explanation: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is characterized by chronic inflammation that is limited to the mucosa and submucosa of the colon and rectum. Because the disease process is strictly confined to these specific layers of the large intestine (starting in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous fashion), a total proctocolectomy (removal of the entire colon and rectum) is curative. This stands in contrast to Crohn's disease, which is a transmural process that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract from the mouth to the anus, making surgery non-curative and reserved only for complications.

distractor_explanations.A: Transmural inflammation is a hallmark of Crohn's disease, not ulcerative colitis. UC involves only the superficial layers (mucosa and submucosa).
distractor_explanations.C: While p-ANCA is found in 60-70% of patients with UC, it is a marker associated with the disease rather than a direct driver produced by a specific 'organ' that can be removed. The cure is based on removing the target tissue (colonic mucosa).
distractor_explanations.D: Skip lesions (discontinuous areas of inflammation) are characteristic of Crohn's disease. UC is characterized by continuous inflammation. Furthermore, the removal of the terminal ileum is not the curative step for UC; removing the colon and rectum is.
distractor_explanations.E: While some extraintestinal manifestations of UC (like peripheral arthritis or erythema nodosum) may improve after colectomy, others, specifically primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) and ankylosing spondylitis, follow a course independent of the bowel disease and do not resolve after surgery.

learning_objective: Total proctocolectomy is curative in ulcerative colitis because the disease is confined to the mucosa and submucosa of the colon and rectum.
key_concept: Surgical management of ulcerative colitis is curative due to the disease's localization to the colonic/rectal mucosa and submucosa.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old female is currently being stabilized in the intensive care unit for an episode of acute pancreatitis. On admission, she presented with sudden-onset, severe epigastric pain radiating to the back and a serum lipase level of 6,200 U/L. An initial right upper quadrant ultrasound demonstrated multiple small gallstones in the gallbladder, no gallbladder wall thickening, and a common bile duct diameter of 5 mm. She was treated with aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation and pain management. On hospital day 5, the patient's pain has resolved, her bowel sounds are active, and she is tolerating a low-fat diet without discomfort. Her vital signs are stable, and she is being prepared for transfer to the general medical floor.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent a recurrence of this patient's condition?

answer_choices.A: Daily administration of ursodeoxycholic acid
answer_choices.C: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy during this hospital admission
answer_choices.D: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy in 6 to 8 weeks after discharge
answer_choices.E: Low-fat diet and weight loss without surgical intervention

explanation: This patient has gallstone pancreatitis, the most common cause of acute pancreatitis in the United States. For patients with mild-to-moderate gallstone pancreatitis, laparoscopic cholecystectomy should be performed during the index (current) hospitalization, usually after the clinical symptoms of pancreatitis have resolved. This 'early' cholecystectomy (ideally within 7 days of presentation) significantly reduces the risk of recurrent biliary events, including recurrent pancreatitis, cholecystitis, and cholangitis, compared to delayed outpatient surgery. Delaying the procedure is associated with a high rate (up to 25-30%) of readmission for biliary complications within the subsequent weeks.

distractor_explanations.A: Ursodeoxycholic acid is used to dissolve cholesterol gallstones in patients who are poor surgical candidates. It is significantly less effective than cholecystectomy and is not the standard of care for preventing recurrence of pancreatitis in a patient who is otherwise stable for surgery.
distractor_explanations.B: ERCP with sphincterotomy is indicated for patients with gallstone pancreatitis who have concurrent acute cholangitis or evidence of a persistent common bile duct obstruction (e.g., worsening jaundice, rising bilirubin). Since this patient's symptoms are resolving and the bile duct was not dilated on ultrasound, ERCP is not the primary preventative step.
distractor_explanations.D: While cholecystectomy may be delayed in cases of severe necrotizing pancreatitis (until inflammation subsides and fluid collections wall off), for mild-to-moderate gallstone pancreatitis, delaying the procedure for 6-8 weeks increases the risk of a recurrent attack before the surgery can take place.
distractor_explanations.E: Dietary modification alone is insufficient to prevent the migration of existing stones. Definitive treatment of the source (the gallbladder) is required to prevent recurrent pancreatitis.

learning_objective: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy should be performed during the same hospital admission for patients with mild-to-moderate gallstone pancreatitis to prevent recurrent biliary complications.
key_concept: Prevention of recurrent gallstone pancreatitis through early cholecystectomy.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 55-year-old female presents to the outpatient clinic for a follow-up visit after being hospitalized one month ago for severe epigastric pain and vomiting. During her hospitalization, serum lipase was 2,800 U/L (Reference: 10–140 U/L). A right upper quadrant ultrasound showed a normal-appearing gallbladder without stones or sludge, and a common bile duct diameter of 4 mm. She has a history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension but does not consume alcohol or tobacco. Her BMI is 34 kg/m². Current laboratory results show a fasting glucose of 142 mg/dL, hemoglobin A1c of 8.2%, total cholesterol of 245 mg/dL, and triglycerides of 1,150 mg/dL. She is currently asymptomatic and follows a standard diabetic diet.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term management to reduce this patient's risk of recurrent pancreatitis?

answer_choices.A: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
answer_choices.C: Fenofibrate therapy
answer_choices.D: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP)
answer_choices.E: Ursodeoxycholic acid therapy

explanation: This patient has a history of acute pancreatitis likely secondary to severe hypertriglyceridemia (HTG), defined as triglycerides >1,000 mg/dL. HTG is the third most common cause of acute pancreatitis (after gallstones and alcohol). In the maintenance phase, the goal is to keep triglyceride levels <500 mg/dL to prevent recurrence. Fibrates (e.g., fenofibrate, gemfibrozil) are the first-line pharmacologic agents for severe HTG because they activate peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-alpha (PPAR-α), which increases the expression of lipoprotein lipase (LPL) and decreases the production of apolipoprotein C-III (an LPL inhibitor), thereby significantly lowering serum triglycerides. The pathophysiology of HTG-induced pancreatitis involves the breakdown of triglycerides into toxic free fatty acids (FFAs) by pancreatic lipase. When FFAs exceed the binding capacity of serum albumin, they form detergent-like micelles that cause direct cytotoxic injury to pancreatic acinar cells and capillary endothelium, leading to ischemia and inflammation.

distractor_explanations.A: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the definitive treatment for gallstone pancreatitis. However, this patient's ultrasound was negative for stones and sludge, making a biliary etiology less likely.
distractor_explanations.B: Atorvastatin is an HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor used primarily to lower LDL cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk. While statins have a modest effect on triglycerides, they are insufficient as monotherapy when triglycerides are >1,000 mg/dL and the primary goal is preventing pancreatitis.
distractor_explanations.D: ERCP is indicated for acute gallstone pancreatitis associated with cholangitis or persistent common bile duct obstruction. It has no role in the management or prevention of hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis.
distractor_explanations.E: Ursodeoxycholic acid is used to dissolve small cholesterol gallstones in patients who are not surgical candidates or to prevent gallstone formation during rapid weight loss. It does not lower triglyceride levels or prevent HTG-induced pancreatitis.

learning_objective: In patients with acute pancreatitis caused by severe hypertriglyceridemia (>1,000 mg/dL), long-term health maintenance focuses on preventing recurrence by maintaining triglyceride levels <500 mg/dL using fibrate therapy and lifestyle modifications.
key_concept: Hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis pathophysiology and prevention.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old female is hospitalized for the management of acute pancreatitis. She presented three days ago with sudden-onset, severe epigastric pain that radiated to her back and was partially relieved by leaning forward. At admission, her serum lipase was 4,200 U/L. A right upper quadrant ultrasound revealed multiple small gallstones within the gallbladder, a common bile duct (CBD) diameter of 4 mm, and no evidence of pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening. She has been treated with aggressive intravenous lactated Ringer's solution and her symptoms have significantly improved. Currently, she is pain-free, afebrile, and has tolerated a low-fat diet for the past 24 hours. Her follow-up serum lipase is 450 U/L (Reference range: 10–140 U/L). The surgical consultant suggests that due to the recent acute inflammation, the patient should be discharged today and return in 6 weeks for an elective outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
question_stem: Which of the following management plans is the most appropriate next step to minimize the risk of a preventable readmission for this patient?

answer_choices.A: Discharge the patient with a prescription for ursodeoxycholic acid and outpatient surgical follow-up in 6 weeks
answer_choices.B: Perform laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the current hospitalization
answer_choices.C: Perform ERCP with sphincterotomy followed by discharge with outpatient cholecystectomy
answer_choices.D: Continue inpatient observation until the serum lipase has returned to within the normal reference range
answer_choices.E: Obtain a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen to ensure resolution of peripancreatic inflammation before proceeding with surgery

explanation: This patient has mild gallstone pancreatitis that has clinically resolved. In patients with mild gallstone pancreatitis, performing a laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same index hospitalization is the standard of care for patient safety and quality improvement. Delaying cholecystectomy (Choice A) is associated with a high risk (up to 25–30%) of recurrent biliary complications, such as recurrent pancreatitis, cholecystitis, or cholangitis, within the first few weeks following discharge. Studies have shown that same-admission cholecystectomy significantly reduces these preventable readmissions without increasing the rate of surgical complications, even if the 'inflammatory' window is technically early.

distractor_explanations.A: Outpatient cholecystectomy in 4–6 weeks was historically recommended to allow inflammation to subside, but current evidence shows this practice leads to high rates of recurrent biliary events and preventable hospital readmissions before the scheduled surgery.
distractor_explanations.C: ERCP with sphincterotomy is indicated for gallstone pancreatitis if there is concurrent acute cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction (e.g., rising bilirubin, dilated CBD). In this patient, the CBD is normal and symptoms have resolved, making ERCP unnecessary and potentially harmful (risk of post-ERCP pancreatitis).
distractor_explanations.D: Serum lipase levels do not correlate with the severity of pancreatitis or the resolution of inflammation. Management decisions, including the initiation of feeding and timing of surgery, should be based on clinical improvement (resolution of pain, ability to tolerate diet) rather than the normalization of laboratory values.
distractor_explanations.E: CT imaging is not required for the diagnosis of acute pancreatitis when clinical and laboratory criteria are met. It is also not indicated to 'clear' a patient for surgery in the setting of mild pancreatitis; imaging is reserved for patients who fail to improve or when complications like necrosis or pseudocysts are suspected.

learning_objective: In patients with mild gallstone pancreatitis, laparoscopic cholecystectomy should be performed during the same index hospitalization to reduce the risk of recurrent biliary complications and preventable readmissions.
key_concept: Timing of cholecystectomy in mild gallstone pancreatitis
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old male is currently hospitalized for the management of community-acquired pneumonia. On his fourth hospital day, he develops new-onset confusion, nausea, and vague mid-abdominal discomfort. He has a history of hypertension, for which he takes hydrochlorothiazide, and osteoporosis, for which he takes daily calcium and vitamin D supplements. He does not consume alcohol. On physical examination, he is lethargic and disoriented. His temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F), blood pressure is 112/74 mmHg, and heart rate is 102/min. Abdominal examination reveals decreased bowel sounds and mild distension with vague epigastric tenderness but no guarding or rebound. Laboratory results show a serum lipase of 1,240 U/L (Reference: 10–160 U/L), alanine aminotransferase (ALT) of 22 U/L, and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) of 28 U/L. A right upper quadrant ultrasound shows a thin-walled gallbladder without stones or sludge and a common bile duct diameter of 4 mm.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluating the etiology of this patient's current condition?

answer_choices.A: Computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen with intravenous contrast
answer_choices.C: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP)
answer_choices.D: Serum calcium and triglyceride levels
answer_choices.E: Viral serologies for mumps and Coxsackievirus

explanation: The patient has acute pancreatitis, diagnosed by the presence of characteristic abdominal pain (though vague in this elderly patient) and a serum lipase >3 times the upper limit of normal. In the evaluation of acute pancreatitis etiology, the most common causes are gallstones and alcohol. Since the right upper quadrant ultrasound was negative for stones/sludge and the patient has no history of alcohol use, the next step is to evaluate for metabolic causes. Hypercalcemia and hypertriglyceridemia (>1000 mg/dL) are well-recognized triggers. This patient has multiple risk factors for hypercalcemia, including his age, thiazide diuretic use (which decreases urinary calcium excretion), and calcium supplementation. In the elderly, acute pancreatitis often presents atypically with vague symptoms, confusion (delirium), or isolated ileus rather than the classic severe, radiating epigastric pain.

distractor_explanations.A: CT of the abdomen with contrast is used to confirm the diagnosis if clinical and lab findings are ambiguous or to assess for complications (e.g., necrosis) if the patient does not improve after 72-96 hours. It is not the best step for determining the underlying metabolic or obstructive etiology.
distractor_explanations.B: ERCP is indicated for acute gallstone pancreatitis with concomitant cholangitis or evidence of persistent common bile duct obstruction. This patient has a normal ultrasound and normal liver function tests, making a biliary cause unlikely.
distractor_explanations.C: MRCP is useful for identifying occult choledocholithiasis when ultrasound is negative but clinical suspicion (e.g., elevated LFTs) remains high. This patient's LFTs are normal, making metabolic workup higher yield than further biliary imaging.
distractor_explanations.E: While certain viruses can cause pancreatitis, they are much less common in the elderly compared to metabolic or drug-induced causes (like thiazides or hypercalcemia).

learning_objective: When the initial workup for acute pancreatitis (ultrasound and alcohol history) is negative, the next best step in determining etiology is to check serum calcium and triglyceride levels.
key_concept: In patients with acute pancreatitis and a negative ultrasound for gallstones, metabolic causes such as hypercalcemia and hypertriglyceridemia should be investigated.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of severe, constant epigastric pain that radiates to her back. She has had several episodes of non-bilious vomiting. The patient has no history of alcohol use or recent trauma but notes a history of occasional right upper quadrant discomfort after fatty meals. Her temperature is 38.7°C (101.7°F), heart rate is 114/min, and blood pressure is 108/64 mmHg. On examination, she appears jaundiced and has significant tenderness in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant without rebound or rigidity. Laboratory studies show a serum lipase of 4,200 U/L (Reference range: 10–160 U/L), alanine aminotransferase (ALT) of 215 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) of 110 U/L, alkaline phosphatase of 310 U/L, and a total bilirubin of 5.2 mg/dL. An ultrasound of the right upper quadrant reveals multiple gallstones, a thickened gallbladder wall, and a common bile duct diameter of 11 mm.
question_stem: In addition to aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 24 hours
answer_choices.C: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography
answer_choices.D: Computed tomography of the abdomen with intravenous contrast
answer_choices.E: Conservative management with serial abdominal examinations and NPO status

explanation: The patient presents with acute pancreatitis (suggested by severe epigastric pain radiating to the back and lipase >3x the upper limit of normal). The elevated ALT (>150 U/L) has a high positive predictive value for a biliary etiology (gallstone pancreatitis). While the initial management of all acute pancreatitis involves aggressive fluid resuscitation and pain control, the presence of Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain) along with a dilated common bile duct (11 mm) and significantly elevated bilirubin (5.2 mg/dL) indicates concomitant acute cholangitis. In the setting of gallstone pancreatitis, urgent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is indicated if there is evidence of acute cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction (e.g., worsening jaundice or rising bilirubin). ERCP allows for the removal of the obstructing stone and decompression of the biliary tree.

distractor_explanations.A: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is indicated for all patients with gallstone pancreatitis to prevent recurrence. However, in mild cases, it is typically performed after the pancreatitis has improved and before discharge. In patients with cholangitis, the biliary obstruction must be addressed first via ERCP.
distractor_explanations.C: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) is a highly sensitive non-invasive test for detecting choledocholithiasis. It is appropriate for patients with 'intermediate risk' of a common bile duct stone. However, this patient is 'high risk' (bilirubin >4, dilated duct, and signs of cholangitis), making ERCP the preferred choice as it is both diagnostic and therapeutic.
distractor_explanations.D: CT of the abdomen with contrast is the gold standard for diagnosing complications of pancreatitis (e.g., necrosis, pseudocysts). However, it is generally not recommended within the first 72–96 hours of presentation unless the diagnosis is uncertain, as early findings of inflammation may not accurately reflect the extent of necrosis.
distractor_explanations.E: Conservative management (fluids, pain control, early enteral nutrition) is the mainstay for most patients with acute pancreatitis. However, it is insufficient here because the patient has an active biliary obstruction and infection (cholangitis) that requires procedural intervention.

learning_objective: Differentiate between uncomplicated gallstone pancreatitis and gallstone pancreatitis with concomitant cholangitis, and recognize that urgent ERCP is required for the latter.
key_concept: Urgent ERCP in gallstone pancreatitis is indicated for patients with concomitant acute cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 54-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic for a health maintenance examination. He has a history of chronic hepatitis C and a 30-year history of heavy alcohol consumption, though he reports abstinence for the past six months. On physical examination, he appears well-nourished. Vital signs are within normal limits. Skin examination reveals several spider angiomata on the upper chest and bilateral palmar erythema. Abdominal examination shows a firm, nodular liver edge 2 cm below the costal margin and a palpable spleen tip. There is no fluid wave or shifting dullness. Laboratory studies are significant for a platelet count of 92,000/mm³, an aspartate aminotransferase (AST) of 58 U/L, an alanine aminotransferase (ALT) of 45 U/L, and an international normalized ratio (INR) of 1.4. An esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) performed 6 months ago showed no evidence of esophageal varices.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the health maintenance of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Prophylactic administration of a non-selective beta-blocker
answer_choices.C: Serum ammonia level measurement every 3 months
answer_choices.D: Daily low-dose spironolactone therapy
answer_choices.E: Repeat esophagogastroduodenoscopy in 5 years

explanation: This patient has clinical and laboratory evidence of compensated cirrhosis (spider angiomata, palmar erythema, nodular liver, splenomegaly, and thrombocytopenia). All patients with cirrhosis, regardless of the etiology or compensation status, are at high risk for developing hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). The standard of care for HCC screening is an abdominal ultrasound every 6 months. Some clinicians also include serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) measurement, although ultrasound is the primary screening modality. Early detection of HCC through screening is vital as it allows for curative treatment options such as resection or liver transplantation.

distractor_explanations.A: Non-selective beta-blockers (e.g., propranolol, nadolol) are used for the primary prophylaxis of variceal hemorrhage in patients with small varices plus high-risk features or in patients with medium-to-large varices. Since this patient's EGD 6 months ago was clear, beta-blockers are not currently indicated.
distractor_explanations.C: Serum ammonia levels are useful in the diagnostic workup of a patient with altered mental status suspected of having hepatic encephalopathy. However, serial ammonia levels have no role in the screening or routine health maintenance of asymptomatic patients with cirrhosis, as levels do not correlate well with the severity of encephalopathy.
distractor_explanations.D: Spironolactone is the first-line diuretic for the management of ascites in cirrhosis. This patient currently has no evidence of ascites (no shifting dullness or peripheral edema); therefore, prophylactic diuretics are not indicated and could cause electrolyte imbalances or dehydration.
distractor_explanations.E: In patients with compensated cirrhosis and no varices on the initial screening EGD, the procedure should be repeated every 2 to 3 years to monitor for the development of varices. A 5-year interval is too long and increases the risk of missing potentially life-threatening varices.

learning_objective: Recognize that all patients with cirrhosis require screening for hepatocellular carcinoma with abdominal ultrasound every 6 months.
key_concept: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) screening in cirrhosis
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by her daughter due to increasing abdominal distension and yellowing of the eyes over the last month. The patient has a history of chronic hepatitis C infection diagnosed two decades ago but has not sought medical care in several years. On physical examination, she appears thin with notable scleral icterus. Her chest reveals several blanched lesions with central red spots and radiating vessels. Her palms are erythematous. The abdomen is significantly distended with a positive fluid wave and several prominent, dilated veins radiating from the umbilicus. Laboratory studies are significant for a platelet count of 88,000/mm³, an INR of 1.8, and a serum albumin of 2.6 g/dL.
question_stem: Which of the following cellular processes is most directly responsible for the structural changes and hepatic fibrosis observed in this patient?

answer_choices.A: Activation of hepatic stellate cells into myofibroblasts
answer_choices.B: Hyperplasia of Kupffer cells in the sinusoids
answer_choices.C: Neutrophilic infiltration and Mallory body formation
answer_choices.D: Proliferation of bile ductular cells in the portal tracts
answer_choices.E: Apoptosis of sinusoidal endothelial cells

explanation: The patient's clinical presentation—jaundice, spider angiomata, palmar erythema, ascites, and caput medusae—is classic for decompensated cirrhosis. The hallmark of cirrhosis is the replacement of normal liver parenchyma with fibrotic tissue and regenerative nodules. The central event in the pathophysiology of liver fibrosis is the activation of hepatic stellate cells (also known as Ito cells). In their quiescent state, these cells reside in the space of Disse and store Vitamin A. In response to chronic liver injury (from hepatitis C, alcohol, or NAFLD), they undergo a transformation into activated myofibroblasts. These activated cells secrete large amounts of collagen (specifically types I and III), which leads to the distortion of hepatic architecture, increased resistance to portal blood flow, and eventual portal hypertension.

distractor_explanations.B: Kupffer cells are resident macrophages of the liver. While they release pro-inflammatory cytokines (like TGF-beta) that help activate stellate cells, they are not the primary cells responsible for the deposition of collagen and fibrotic tissue.
distractor_explanations.C: Neutrophilic infiltration and Mallory-Denk bodies (damaged cytokeratin filaments) are classic histological features of acute alcoholic hepatitis, rather than the chronic process of cirrhotic fibrosis itself.
distractor_explanations.D: Bile ductular proliferation (ductular reaction) occurs in various forms of liver injury, particularly cholestatic diseases, but it is not the primary mechanism of the generalized fibrosis seen in cirrhosis.
distractor_explanations.E: While damage to sinusoidal endothelial cells occurs (leading to 'capillarization' of the sinusoids), it is the subsequent activation of stellate cells in the space of Disse that leads to the fibrotic deposition characterizing cirrhosis.

learning_objective: Recognize that the activation of hepatic stellate (Ito) cells into collagen-producing myofibroblasts is the primary pathophysiologic mechanism of liver fibrosis and cirrhosis.
key_concept: Hepatic stellate cell activation is the central driver of liver fibrosis.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old female with a history of cirrhosis secondary to nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) presents to the outpatient clinic for a routine follow-up. She reports progressive abdominal distension and mild fatigue but denies fever, abdominal pain, or confusion. Her current medications include spironolactone 100 mg and furosemide 40 mg daily. Physical examination reveals spider angiomata on the chest, icteric sclera, and a distended abdomen with shifting dullness. There is no asterixis. Laboratory studies show a sodium of 131 mEq/L, creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL), and a total bilirubin of 2.2 mg/dL. An esophagogastroduodenoscopy performed six weeks ago showed no esophageal or gastric varices. A diagnostic paracentesis is performed, yielding clear yellow fluid with a total protein level of 1.1 g/dL and a polymorphonuclear leukocyte (PMN) count of 65/mm³.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the preventative management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Initiation of daily oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
answer_choices.B: Initiation of daily oral lactulose titrated to three soft bowel movements
answer_choices.C: Initiation of daily oral propranolol
answer_choices.D: Administration of intravenous albumin (1.5 g/kg)
answer_choices.E: Repeat paracentesis in 48 hours

explanation: This patient has compensated cirrhosis with new-onset or worsening ascites and meets the specific criteria for primary prophylaxis against spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP). The criteria for primary SBP prophylaxis include an ascitic fluid total protein level < 1.5 g/dL, plus at least one of the following: serum creatinine ≥ 1.2 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen ≥ 25 mg/dL, serum sodium ≤ 132 mEq/L, or Child-Pugh score ≥ 9 with a bilirubin ≥ 3 mg/dL. This patient meets the criteria due to her low ascitic protein (1.1 g/dL) and concurrent renal/electrolyte dysfunction (Cr 1.4, Na 131). Appropriate prophylactic regimens include daily oral fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin) or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.

distractor_explanations.A: Correct answer.
distractor_explanations.B: Lactulose is used for the treatment and secondary prophylaxis of hepatic encephalopathy (after a first episode has occurred). It is not indicated for primary prophylaxis in patients who have never had an episode of encephalopathy.
distractor_explanations.C: Non-selective beta-blockers like propranolol are used for the primary prophylaxis of variceal hemorrhage. However, this patient had a recent EGD showing no varices, so beta-blocker therapy is not currently indicated.
distractor_explanations.D: Intravenous albumin (1.5 g/kg at diagnosis and 1.0 g/kg on day 3) is indicated during the treatment of an active SBP infection to reduce the risk of hepatorenal syndrome. It is also used during large-volume paracentesis (>5L) but is not used for primary SBP prophylaxis.
distractor_explanations.E: A repeat paracentesis is typically reserved for patients with SBP who are not responding to antibiotic therapy (showing less than a 25% decrease in PMN count after 48 hours). Since this patient does not have SBP (PMN count < 250/mm³), repeat testing is not indicated.

learning_objective: Identify the criteria for primary prophylaxis of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in patients with cirrhosis (ascitic protein < 1.5 g/dL plus renal or hepatic failure).
key_concept: Primary prophylaxis for SBP is indicated in patients with ascitic fluid protein < 1.5 g/dL and significant renal (Cr ≥ 1.2, BUN ≥ 25, or Na ≤ 132) or hepatic dysfunction (Child-Pugh ≥ 9 and bilirubin ≥ 3).
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 67-year-old female presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal swelling and a 5-kg (11-lb) weight loss. She also reports easy fatigability and mild shortness of breath when walking. Her past medical history is significant for a BMI of 38 kg/m², type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, she has a tense, distended abdomen with shifting dullness and a positive fluid wave. There is mild scleral icterus but no spider angiomata or palmar erythema. Her jugular venous pressure is 7 cm H2O (normal < 8 cm H2O). Heart sounds are normal, and lungs are clear to auscultation. Diagnostic paracentesis is performed, yielding straw-colored fluid. Laboratory analysis of the ascitic fluid shows an albumin level of 1.5 g/dL and a total protein level of 1.7 g/dL. Simultaneously, her serum albumin is 3.3 g/dL.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the health maintenance of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Peritoneal fluid adenosine deaminase measurement
answer_choices.C: Transthoracic echocardiography
answer_choices.D: Abdominal ultrasound every 6 months
answer_choices.E: 24-hour urine protein collection

explanation: This patient presents with ascites and signs of chronic liver disease (scleral icterus, fatigue) in the setting of metabolic syndrome (obesity, diabetes), making non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) leading to cirrhosis the most likely diagnosis. The serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) is calculated as (Serum Albumin - Ascitic Albumin). Here, SAAG = 3.3 - 1.5 = 1.8 g/dL. A SAAG ≥ 1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension (e.g., cirrhosis, congestive heart failure, Budd-Chiari syndrome). To differentiate within this group, the ascitic total protein is used: levels < 2.5 g/dL (as seen in this patient, 1.7 g/dL) are characteristic of cirrhosis, whereas levels ≥ 2.5 g/dL suggest a post-hepatic cause like heart failure. Once a diagnosis of cirrhosis is established, patients require lifelong screening for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) using abdominal ultrasound every 6 months (with or without alpha-fetoprotein).

distractor_explanations.A: Adenosine deaminase (ADA) is used to screen for tuberculous peritonitis. However, tuberculosis typically causes an exudative ascites with a SAAG < 1.1 g/dL and high protein levels.
distractor_explanations.B: Cytology is the primary test for peritoneal carcinomatosis. While this patient has weight loss, malignancy usually presents with a SAAG < 1.1 g/dL (unless there are concurrent liver metastases causing portal hypertension) and a high ascitic protein level (≥ 2.5 g/dL).
distractor_explanations.C: Transthoracic echocardiography would be indicated if congestive heart failure (CHF) or constrictive pericarditis were suspected. While CHF causes a high SAAG (≥ 1.1), it is associated with elevated jugular venous pressure and high ascitic protein (≥ 2.5 g/dL).
distractor_explanations.E: Nephrotic syndrome causes ascites due to hypoalbuminemia, but it typically presents with a SAAG < 1.1 g/dL (as there is no portal hypertension) and a low total protein level.

learning_objective: Differentiate causes of ascites using the serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) and ascitic total protein, and identify the need for HCC screening in patients with cirrhosis.
key_concept: A SAAG ≥ 1.1 g/dL indicates portal hypertension. Within that category, an ascitic protein < 2.5 g/dL points to cirrhosis, while an ascitic protein ≥ 2.5 g/dL points to cardiac causes or Budd-Chiari syndrome.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old Hispanic male with a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and obesity presents to the emergency department for evaluation of progressive shortness of breath over the last four months. He notes that his breathing is significantly worse when he is sitting in a chair or walking but improves almost immediately upon lying flat in bed. He denies chest pain, cough, or leg swelling. Physical examination reveals a BMI of 35 kg/m², several spider angiomata on the upper chest, and a distended abdomen with a positive fluid wave. Pulse oximetry shows an oxygen saturation of 89% while the patient is seated and 95% when the patient is in the supine position. Laboratory studies show a platelet count of 105,000/mm³, total bilirubin of 2.6 mg/dL, and an albumin of 2.9 g/dL. A chest X-ray shows no acute infiltrates or cardiomegaly.
question_stem: Which of the following is the next best step to confirm the most likely underlying cause of this patient's respiratory symptoms?

answer_choices.A: CT angiography of the chest
answer_choices.C: Right heart catheterization
answer_choices.D: Pulmonary function testing with lung volumes
answer_choices.E: Therapeutic large-volume paracentesis

explanation: The patient presents with signs of chronic liver disease (spider angiomata, thrombocytopenia, jaundice, and ascites), likely due to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) given his metabolic risk factors. His respiratory symptoms are characteristic of Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS). HPS is defined by the triad of liver disease, increased alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient, and intrapulmonary vascular dilatations (IPVDs). The classic clinical finding is platypnea (shortness of breath that worsens when upright) and orthodeoxia (a decrease in arterial oxygen saturation when moving from a supine to an upright position). This occurs because gravity-dependent dilation of vessels in the lung bases increases shunting when the patient is upright. The next best step to confirm the diagnosis is contrast-enhanced transthoracic echocardiography with agitated saline (bubble study). In a positive study, microbubbles appear in the left atrium after a delay of 3–6 cardiac cycles, indicating intrapulmonary shunting (as opposed to an intracardiac shunt where bubbles appear within 1–2 cycles).

distractor_explanations.A: CT angiography is used to diagnose pulmonary embolism (PE). While PE causes dyspnea and hypoxemia, it does not typically present with the positional platypnea-orthodeoxia seen in this patient.
distractor_explanations.C: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing portopulmonary hypertension (POPH). While POPH is associated with cirrhosis, it typically presents with exertional dyspnea and signs of right heart failure (e.g., loud P2, JVD), rather than platypnea-orthodeoxia.
distractor_explanations.D: Pulmonary function testing in HPS may show a decreased DLCO (diffusing capacity of the lungs for carbon monoxide), but it is non-specific and does not confirm the presence of intrapulmonary vascular dilatations as well as a bubble study or a technetium-99m labeled macroaggregated albumin scan.
distractor_explanations.E: Large-volume paracentesis may improve dyspnea caused by the restrictive effect of massive ascites on the diaphragm. However, ascites-related dyspnea typically worsens when lying flat (orthopnea), which is the opposite of this patient's presentation.

learning_objective: Recognize Hepatopulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in a patient with cirrhosis and confirm the diagnosis using contrast-enhanced echocardiography with agitated saline.
key_concept: Hepatopulmonary Syndrome is characterized by platypnea-orthodeoxia and intrapulmonary shunting due to nitric oxide-mediated vascular dilation in the setting of portal hypertension.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old female presents to the outpatient clinic for a routine health maintenance visit. She is asymptomatic and reports no changes in bowel habits, hematochezia, or unintentional weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable except for a colonoscopy performed at age 46, which was normal. Her family history is significant: her father was diagnosed with colorectal cancer at age 42, her older sister was diagnosed with endometrial cancer at age 44, and a paternal uncle was diagnosed with colorectal cancer at age 47. Physical examination, including abdominal and digital rectal exams, is normal. Vital signs are within normal limits.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in screening for this patient, and which molecular mechanism is responsible for the pathogenesis of her likely condition?

answer_choices.A: Colonoscopy now; DNA mismatch repair deficiency
answer_choices.C: Colonoscopy in 8 years; hypermethylation of the MLH1 promoter
answer_choices.D: Annual fecal immunochemical testing; inactivation of the APC gene
answer_choices.E: Colonoscopy now; loss of heterozygosity of the TP53 gene

explanation: This patient's family history meets the Amsterdam II criteria for Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): at least three relatives with Lynch-associated cancers (colorectal, endometrial, small bowel, ureter/renal pelvis), one of whom is a first-degree relative of the other two, involving at least two successive generations, with at least one diagnosis before age 50. Lynch syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition caused by germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2). This deficiency leads to microsatellite instability (MSI) and an accelerated progression from normal mucosa to carcinoma, often within 1-2 years (compared to 10-15 years in the sporadic adenoma-carcinoma sequence). Consequently, screening colonoscopy is recommended every 1-2 years starting at age 20-25 (or 2-5 years before the earliest diagnosis in the family). Since the patient is 48 and her last colonoscopy was 2 years ago (at age 46), she is due for screening now.

distractor_explanations.A: This is the correct answer. The patient has Lynch syndrome, requiring colonoscopy every 1-2 years due to DNA mismatch repair deficiency.
distractor_explanations.B: A 5-year interval (colonoscopy in 3 years, given her last was 2 years ago) is appropriate for patients with one first-degree relative diagnosed with colorectal cancer before age 60. Chromosomal instability is the hallmark of the sporadic adenoma-carcinoma sequence (APC/KRAS/TP53), not Lynch syndrome.
distractor_explanations.C: A 10-year interval (colonoscopy in 8 years, given her last was 2 years ago) is for average-risk patients. Hypermethylation of the MLH1 promoter is the mechanism for sporadic (non-germline) MSI-high colorectal cancers, often associated with the serrated polyp pathway, but not Lynch syndrome.
distractor_explanations.D: Fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) is an acceptable screening tool for average-risk individuals but is insufficient for high-risk patients like those with Lynch syndrome. Inactivation of the APC gene is the initiating event in the chromosomal instability (sporadic) pathway.
distractor_explanations.E: While 'colonoscopy now' is the correct timing, loss of heterozygosity of the TP53 gene is a late-stage event in the sporadic adenoma-carcinoma sequence, not the primary driver of Lynch syndrome.

learning_objective: Recognize the clinical criteria for Lynch syndrome and understand that it requires frequent colonoscopic surveillance (every 1-2 years) due to defective DNA mismatch repair genes.
key_concept: Lynch syndrome involves germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes leading to microsatellite instability and accelerated colorectal carcinogenesis.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of progressively worsening crampy abdominal pain, nausea, and several episodes of vomiting. She has not passed flatus or had a bowel movement in 48 hours. Over the past six months, she has noted significant fatigue and shortness of breath while climbing stairs, which she assumed was due to heavy menses. Her past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Her father was diagnosed with colon cancer at age 44, and her older sister underwent a hysterectomy for endometrial cancer at age 37. On physical examination, the patient is tachycardic (106/min) and her abdomen is distended with high-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds and diffuse tenderness to palpation. Laboratory results show a hemoglobin of 8.8 g/dL and an MCV of 74 fL. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals a 5.5-cm mass at the ileocecal valve with proximal small bowel dilation and air-fluid levels.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

answer_choices.A: Familial adenomatous polyposis
answer_choices.C: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
answer_choices.D: Sporadic colorectal adenocarcinoma
answer_choices.E: Gardner syndrome

explanation: The patient presents with an acute large bowel obstruction and chronic iron deficiency anemia (microcytic anemia, fatigue) at a young age (35), which is highly suspicious for a hereditary colorectal cancer syndrome. The family history is the key: she has two first-degree relatives with Lynch-associated cancers (father with colon cancer at 44 and sister with endometrial cancer at 37). This fulfills the Amsterdam II criteria (the '3-2-1 rule': at least 3 relatives with Lynch-associated cancer, involving at least 2 generations, with at least 1 diagnosed before age 50). Lynch syndrome (Hereditary Non-Polyposis Colorectal Cancer, or HNPCC) is caused by germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), leading to microsatellite instability (MSI). It typically presents with right-sided (proximal) colon cancers and is associated with a high risk of extracolonic malignancies, most notably endometrial cancer.

distractor_explanations.A: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is caused by an APC gene mutation and is characterized by hundreds to thousands of adenomatous polyps beginning in adolescence. While it causes early-onset CRC, the lack of reported polyposis and the presence of endometrial cancer in the sister point more strongly toward Lynch syndrome.
distractor_explanations.C: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is characterized by hamartomatous polyps throughout the GI tract and mucocutaneous hyperpigmentation (melanotic macules on the lips and buccal mucosa). While it increases the risk of various cancers, it does not fit the Amsterdam criteria pattern seen here.
distractor_explanations.D: Sporadic colorectal adenocarcinoma usually occurs in older patients (typically >45-50) and follows the adenoma-carcinoma sequence (APC-KRAS-TP53). While the incidence is rising in younger populations, the strong family history of early-onset colon and endometrial cancer makes a hereditary syndrome far more likely.
distractor_explanations.E: Gardner syndrome is a variant of FAP that includes extracolonic manifestations such as osteomas, desmoid tumors, and sebaceous cysts. There is no evidence of these findings in this patient, and it would still involve the polyposis associated with FAP.

learning_objective: Recognize Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) in a young patient with colorectal cancer and a family history of colon and extracolonic (e.g., endometrial) malignancies.
key_concept: Lynch syndrome is the most common hereditary colorectal cancer syndrome, characterized by DNA mismatch repair deficiency and an increased risk of right-sided colon, endometrial, and ovarian cancers.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old male is admitted to the inpatient ward for management of a right femoral shaft fracture sustained in a motor vehicle accident. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. He is currently recovering well post-fixation and is preparing for discharge to a rehabilitation facility. During a health maintenance discussion, the patient mentions that his father was diagnosed with colon cancer at the age of 44. The patient has no history of hematochezia, abdominal pain, or changes in bowel habits. Physical examination is unremarkable except for the surgical site. A recent complete blood count shows a hemoglobin of 14.8 g/dL and a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for colorectal cancer screening in this patient?

answer_choices.A: Screening colonoscopy beginning at age 40 and every 10 years thereafter
answer_choices.B: Screening colonoscopy beginning at age 45 and every 10 years thereafter
answer_choices.C: Screening colonoscopy now and every 5 years thereafter
answer_choices.D: Annual fecal immunochemical test (FIT) starting now
answer_choices.E: Fecal DNA testing (Cologuard) every 3 years starting at age 45

explanation: In patients with a first-degree relative (FDR) diagnosed with colorectal cancer (CRC) before the age of 60, screening should begin at age 40 or 10 years before the age of the relative's diagnosis, whichever comes first. In this case, the father was diagnosed at age 44, meaning the patient should have started screening at age 34 (44 - 10 = 34). Since the patient is currently 35, he is already due for his initial screening. Furthermore, the recommended interval for high-risk individuals (those with an FDR < 60) is every 5 years, rather than the standard 10 years used for average-risk individuals.

distractor_explanations.A: Screening at age 40 with a 10-year interval is recommended for patients with a single first-degree relative diagnosed with CRC at age 60 or older. Because this patient's father was diagnosed at 44, more aggressive screening is required.
distractor_explanations.B: Screening at age 45 and every 10 years is the standard recommendation for average-risk individuals. This patient is high-risk due to his family history.
distractor_explanations.C: Correct. Screening should have started at age 34 (10 years before his father's diagnosis) and should be repeated every 5 years.
distractor_explanations.D: While FIT is a valid screening tool for average-risk patients, it is not the preferred primary screening method for individuals at increased risk due to family history. Colonoscopy is the gold standard for high-risk surveillance.
distractor_explanations.E: Fecal DNA testing is an alternative for average-risk individuals but is not recommended for those with a strong family history of CRC.

learning_objective: Identify the appropriate colorectal cancer screening schedule for patients with a first-degree relative diagnosed before age 60.
key_concept: Patients with a first-degree relative diagnosed with colorectal cancer before age 60 should undergo colonoscopy starting at age 40 or 10 years earlier than the relative's diagnosis (whichever comes first), with an interval of every 5 years.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 1-week history of low-grade fever, night sweats, and progressive fatigue. He has a history of hypertension and osteoarthritis but has no significant surgical history. He has never undergone a screening colonoscopy. On physical examination, his temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), heart rate is 92/min, and blood pressure is 132/82 mmHg. A new grade III/VI holosystolic murmur is heard at the apex radiating to the axilla. His abdomen is soft and non-tender without masses or distension. Lab results reveal a hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL and a mean corpuscular volume of 76 fL. Multiple blood cultures are drawn and subsequently grow Streptococcus gallolyticus. An echocardiogram reveals a 1-cm vegetation on the mitral valve.
question_stem: In addition to initiating intravenous antibiotics, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Fecal immunochemical testing (FIT)
answer_choices.C: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast
answer_choices.D: Colonoscopy
answer_choices.E: Repeat blood cultures in 2 weeks

explanation: This patient presents with infective endocarditis (IE) caused by Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly known as Streptococcus bovis biotype I). There is a very strong and well-documented association between S. gallolyticus bacteremia/endocarditis and colorectal cancer (CRC) or other colonic lesions (e.g., adenomatous polyps). The bacteria are thought to enter the bloodstream through the disrupted colonic mucosa provided by the malignancy. Even in the absence of overt gastrointestinal symptoms, any patient with S. gallolyticus bacteremia must undergo a colonoscopy to screen for an occult colorectal malignancy. While the USPSTF generally recommends stopping routine screening at age 75-85 depending on individual factors, the discovery of S. gallolyticus provides a specific clinical indication for diagnostic screening regardless of age.

distractor_explanations.A: Fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) is a screening tool for average-risk individuals. Because S. gallolyticus bacteremia confers a very high risk of underlying malignancy, a definitive diagnostic procedure (colonoscopy) is required even if stool tests are negative.
distractor_explanations.B: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a tumor marker used for monitoring response to treatment and detecting recurrence in patients already diagnosed with CRC. It is not used for initial screening or diagnosis due to low sensitivity and specificity.
distractor_explanations.C: A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis is useful for the staging of colorectal cancer (e.g., detecting liver metastases) but it is not sensitive enough to be the primary screening tool for identifying intraluminal colonic lesions or small adenomas.
distractor_explanations.E: While repeat blood cultures are necessary to document the clearance of bacteremia in the management of infective endocarditis, this does not address the high risk of an underlying colonic source that must be evaluated.

learning_objective: Identify Streptococcus gallolyticus bacteremia/endocarditis as a high-risk clinical marker requiring colonoscopy to evaluate for occult colorectal cancer.
key_concept: The association between Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly Streptococcus bovis) and colorectal cancer requires mandatory gastrointestinal workup.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 8-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure that lasted 2 minutes. His mother reports that over the past month, the boy has complained of headaches that are most severe in the morning and occasionally associated with nausea and vomiting. Physical examination is notable for papilledema, truncal ataxia, and a slight head tilt. The boy's family history is significant for his father, who was diagnosed with thousands of colonic polyps at age 24 and underwent a prophylactic total proctocolectomy to prevent colorectal cancer. The boy has not had any prior medical screening. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Genetic testing for the APC gene mutation
answer_choices.C: Screening colonoscopy
answer_choices.D: Electroencephalogram (EEG)
answer_choices.E: Lumbar puncture with cerebrospinal fluid analysis

explanation: This patient's family history (father with thousands of polyps and prophylactic colectomy) is diagnostic of Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP), an autosomal dominant condition caused by a mutation in the APC tumor suppressor gene. FAP carries a 100% risk of colorectal cancer if untreated. Turcot syndrome is a clinical variant where FAP (or Lynch syndrome) is associated with primary central nervous system (CNS) tumors. Specifically, patients with the APC mutation (FAP-associated Turcot) are at high risk for medulloblastomas, which typically occur in the first decade of life and often arise in the cerebellar vermis (explaining the truncal ataxia). The patient's symptoms (morning headaches, vomiting, seizure, papilledema) indicate increased intracranial pressure, likely from a posterior fossa tumor. Contrast-enhanced MRI is the gold standard for evaluating suspected CNS malignancies and is the next best step in management.

distractor_explanations.A: Genetic testing for the APC mutation is the standard first step for evaluating asymptomatic children of an affected parent, typically by age 10. However, in the setting of acute neurological symptoms and signs of increased intracranial pressure, the immediate priority is neuroimaging.
distractor_explanations.C: In children with FAP, screening with annual flexible sigmoidoscopy or colonoscopy typically begins between ages 10 and 12. While this patient will eventually require colonoscopy if his APC status is positive, it is not the most appropriate immediate step for a patient presenting with an acute seizure and signs of a brain tumor.
distractor_explanations.D: An EEG is useful in the workup of seizures to identify epileptiform discharges; however, it does not identify the underlying structural cause (e.g., a tumor) and should only be performed after neuroimaging has ruled out a mass lesion in a patient with focal signs or signs of increased intracranial pressure.
distractor_explanations.E: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in patients with signs of significantly increased intracranial pressure (e.g., papilledema) and a suspected obstructive mass lesion, as it can precipitate life-threatening brain herniation.

learning_objective: Recognize Turcot syndrome as a variant of Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) and identify neuroimaging as the next best step for an at-risk child presenting with signs of a central nervous system tumor.
key_concept: Turcot syndrome (FAP + medulloblastoma or Lynch + glioblastoma).
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 67-year-old male presents to a community health center with a 2-day history of anorexia and vague, dull, aching pain centered around his umbilicus. He reports that over the last 12 hours, the pain has become significantly sharper and has relocated toward his right flank and back. He has not had a bowel movement in 24 hours but denies diarrhea or vomiting. His medical history is significant only for hypertension. On physical examination, his temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), heart rate is 98/min, and blood pressure is 134/86 mmHg. Abdominal examination reveals minimal tenderness upon deep palpation of McBurney’s point and no guarding of the anterior abdominal wall. However, the patient experiences exquisite sharp pain in the right lower quadrant and flank when he is placed in the left lateral decubitus position and his right hip is passively extended. Laboratory studies are remarkable for a leukocyte count of 14,800/mm³ with 82% segmented neutrophils and 4% bands.
question_stem: Which of the following best explains the neuroanatomical mechanism for the transition in this patient’s pain characteristics?

answer_choices.A: Inflammatory irritation of the posterior parietal peritoneum innervated by somatic A-delta fibers
answer_choices.B: Distension of the appendiceal wall stimulating visceral afferent C-fibers that converge at the T10 spinal level
answer_choices.C: Activation of the genitofemoral nerve due to the proximity of the inflamed appendix to the ureter
answer_choices.D: Referred pain from the visceral peritoneum mediated by the vagus nerve and celiac ganglion
answer_choices.E: Reflexive spasm of the iliopsoas muscle causing compressive neuropathy of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve

explanation: This patient presents with acute appendicitis. The classic migration of pain begins with vague, dull periumbilical pain (visceral pain) that later becomes sharp and well-localized (somatic pain). The initial visceral pain is caused by luminal obstruction and distension, which stimulates visceral afferent C-fibers. These fibers travel with sympathetic nerves to the T10 spinal level, resulting in referred pain to the periumbilical dermatome. As inflammation progresses to involve the serosa and eventually the parietal peritoneum, the pain becomes somatic. Somatic pain is mediated by A-delta fibers, which are myelinated, fast-conducting, and allow for precise localization. In this patient, the negative McBurney’s point tenderness and positive psoas sign (pain on hip extension) indicate a retrocecal appendix. In this anatomical variant, the inflamed appendix irritates the posterior parietal peritoneum overlying the psoas muscle rather than the anterior parietal peritoneum, explaining the localization to the flank and the specific physical exam findings.

distractor_explanations.A: This correctly identifies the mechanism for the transition to sharp, localized pain (somatic A-delta fibers) and accounts for the retrocecal location (posterior parietal peritoneum).
distractor_explanations.B: This describes the mechanism of the initial periumbilical pain (visceral referred pain), but it does not explain the transition to sharp, localized flank pain.
distractor_explanations.C: While an inflamed appendix can irritate the ureter (causing mild pyuria), the transition of pain from the umbilicus to the flank in this clinical context is characteristic of the visceral-to-somatic shift in appendicitis, not genitofemoral nerve activation.
distractor_explanations.D: The visceral afferents from the appendix travel with the sympathetic fibers to the T10-T11 spinal cord segments via the lesser splanchnic nerves, not the vagus nerve. The vagus nerve carries parasympathetic fibers and is not the primary mediator of appendiceal pain.
distractor_explanations.E: The psoas sign is caused by the inflamed appendix contacting the parietal peritoneum/fascia overlying the psoas muscle; it is not due to a reflexive spasm or compression of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (which provides sensation to the lateral thigh).

learning_objective: The migration of pain in acute appendicitis reflects the transition from visceral pain (mediated by C-fibers) to somatic pain (mediated by A-delta fibers) as the inflammation involves the parietal peritoneum.
key_concept: Neuroanatomical basis of appendiceal pain migration and retrocecal presentation.
subject: Surgery

vignette: A 72-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of worsening right lower quadrant abdominal pain, anorexia, and a low-grade fever. She has not sought medical care in several years and her last colonoscopy was 20 years ago. On examination, her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), heart rate is 96/min, and blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg. Physical examination reveals a palpable, tender mass in the right lower quadrant with overlying mild guarding, but no generalized peritonitis. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 17,200/mm³ with 85% neutrophils. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis reveals a 4.5-cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the right iliac fossa associated with a thickened, dilated appendix. The patient is admitted, started on intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam, and undergoes successful percutaneous drainage of the collection by interventional radiology.
question_stem: In addition to considering an interval appendectomy in 6 to 8 weeks, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the health maintenance for this patient?

answer_choices.A: Colonoscopy
answer_choices.B: Measurement of serum carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) level
answer_choices.C: Lifelong prophylactic oral antibiotic therapy
answer_choices.D: Repeat CT scan of the abdomen in 2 weeks
answer_choices.E: Technetium-99m labeled RBC scan

explanation: This elderly patient presented with complicated appendicitis (perforation with abscess formation). In patients older than 40–50 years who present with appendicitis, especially those with atypical features or complications like a phlegmon or abscess, the clinician must be highly suspicious of an underlying colorectal malignancy. A cecal adenocarcinoma can obstruct the appendiceal orifice, leading to secondary appendicitis. Because the inflammatory process makes immediate colonoscopy or surgical exploration technically difficult and prone to complications (e.g., bowel injury), the standard approach is 'cool-down' management with antibiotics and/or drainage, followed by a colonoscopy in 6–8 weeks once the inflammation has subsided to rule out an underlying malignancy.

distractor_explanations.A: Colonoscopy is the correct next step to screen for underlying cecal cancer, which can present as appendicitis in older adults.
distractor_explanations.B: CEA is a marker used for monitoring the recurrence of known colorectal cancer; it is not a recommended screening tool for primary diagnosis due to low sensitivity and specificity.
distractor_explanations.C: There is no indication for long-term or lifelong antibiotic therapy after the resolution of an appendiceal abscess.
distractor_explanations.D: While a repeat CT might be used to ensure the abscess has resolved, it is not a substitute for colonoscopy, as it cannot definitively exclude a mucosal or early-stage cecal malignancy.
distractor_explanations.E: A technetium-99m labeled RBC scan is used to localize the site of active gastrointestinal bleeding and has no role in the screening or management of appendicitis or cecal cancer.

learning_objective: In patients older than 40-50 years presenting with acute appendicitis, a follow-up colonoscopy should be performed after the resolution of inflammation to rule out an underlying cecal malignancy.
key_concept: Elderly patients with appendicitis are at increased risk for underlying colorectal cancer; post-inflammatory screening with colonoscopy is mandatory.
subject: Surgery

vignette: A 72-year-old male presents to a community health center with a 3-day history of vague, dull abdominal discomfort. He initially felt the pain around his umbilicus, but for the last 24 hours, it has become more constant in the right lower quadrant. He reports a loss of appetite but denies any significant nausea or vomiting. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, his temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), heart rate is 92/min, and blood pressure is 138/84 mmHg. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness to palpation in the right lower quadrant with mild voluntary guarding. There is no rebound tenderness or palpable mass. Laboratory studies are significant for a leukocyte count of 12,200/mm³ with 78% neutrophils.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_choices.A: Abdominal ultrasound
answer_choices.B: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
