In contrast, thyroiditis shows low/suppressed uptake because the gland is damaged and the suppressed TSH (due to the leakage of hormone) prevents new iodine uptake.

distractor_explanations.A: Anti-TPO antibodies are common in both Graves disease (approx. 75-80%) and Hashimoto thyroiditis/silent thyroiditis (>90%). Because they are present in both conditions, they are not a reliable differentiator.
distractor_explanations.B: Serum thyroglobulin is a marker of thyroid tissue and activity. It is elevated in Graves disease (high turnover/production) and thyroiditis (leakage from the follicle). It is only low in exogenous (factitious) thyrotoxicosis, where ingested hormone suppresses the endogenous gland's activity.
distractor_explanations.C: An increased ESR is characteristic of subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis. However, de Quervain thyroiditis typically presents with a very tender, painful goiter, usually following a viral illness, unlike this patient's non-tender gland.
distractor_explanations.E: While Graves disease does show increased vascularity (the 'thyroid inferno' on Doppler), this choice is less definitive than RAIU in most clinical algorithms for distinguishing the etiology of hyperthyroidism, as Doppler findings can sometimes be operator-dependent or ambiguous in mild cases.

learning_objective: Distinguish Graves disease from thyroiditis by using radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) to differentiate increased hormone synthesis (high uptake) from the release of pre-formed hormone (low uptake).
key_concept: Graves disease presents with high RAIU due to autoantibody-mediated stimulation of hormone synthesis, whereas thyroiditis presents with low RAIU due to follicular damage and TSH suppression.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 8-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her mother due to concerns about her daughter's "racing heart" and "bulging eyes." Over the past two months, the mother notes the child has been increasingly irritable, hyperactive, and has lost 5 pounds despite a significantly increased appetite. The patient also complains of feeling hot even in air-conditioned rooms. On physical examination, her heart rate is 128/min and blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg. There is a visible, diffusely enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. A fine tremor is noted when she holds her hands out. Significant proptosis and lid lag are present bilaterally. No discrete thyroid nodules are palpated.
question_stem: Which of the following describes the most likely underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition?

answer_choices.A: Autoantibody-mediated destruction of thyroid follicular cells
answer_choices.B: Binding of stimulatory antibodies to the thyrotropin receptor
answer_choices.C: Release of preformed thyroid hormone following a viral upper respiratory infection
answer_choices.D: Autonomous production of thyroid hormone by a hyperfunctioning nodule
answer_choices.E: Ectopic thyroid hormone production by a mature cystic teratoma

explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of Graves disease, the most common cause of hyperthyroidism in children and adolescents. The hallmark findings include tachycardia, weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, tremor, diffuse goiter, and ophthalmopathy (proptosis/lid lag). The underlying pathophysiology of Graves disease is a Type II (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity reaction in which B-cells produce thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI). These autoantibodies bind to and activate the thyrotropin (TSH) receptor on thyroid follicular cells, leading to diffuse thyroid hyperplasia and excessive synthesis/secretion of T3 and T4. The proptosis is caused by T-cell activation and TSI-mediated stimulation of TSH receptors on orbital fibroblasts, leading to glycosaminoglycan accumulation and retro-orbital inflammation.

distractor_explanations.A: This describes Hashimoto thyroiditis, the most common cause of hypothyroidism. While it may occasionally present with a transient hyperthyroid phase ('Hashitoxicosis'), it is characterized by lymphocytic infiltration and destruction of the gland, not the stimulatory mechanism seen in Graves.
distractor_explanations.C: This describes subacute granulomatous (de Quervain) thyroiditis. It typically presents with a painful, tender goiter following a viral illness and is characterized by the release of preformed hormone, not the diffuse hyperplasia and proptosis seen in Graves.
distractor_explanations.D: This describes a toxic multinodular goiter or a toxic adenoma. While these cause hyperthyroidism, they present with one or more palpable nodules rather than the diffuse, smooth goiter seen in this child, and they are not associated with exophthalmos.
distractor_explanations.E: This describes struma ovarii, a rare cause of hyperthyroidism where ectopic thyroid tissue within an ovarian teratoma produces excess hormone. It would not cause a diffuse goiter or proptosis.

learning_objective: Recognize Graves disease as the most common cause of pediatric hyperthyroidism and understand its mechanism as a Type II hypersensitivity reaction involving stimulatory autoantibodies against the TSH receptor.
key_concept: Graves disease is caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) that act as agonists at the TSH receptor.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the outpatient clinic by his mother because of worsening restlessness and declining school performance over the past 4 months. His teacher reports he is "constantly in motion" and struggles to finish tasks. The mother notes he has become increasingly irritable, complains of feeling hot even in cool environments, and has lost 2.3 kg despite an insatiable appetite. Both parents smoke one pack of cigarettes per day and smoke indoors. On examination, the patient's heart rate is 122/min and blood pressure is 112/66 mmHg. He has a fine tremor of his outstretched hands. The thyroid gland is symmetrically enlarged, smooth, and non-tender, with a faint bruit heard on auscultation. There is a perceptible stare and mild lid lag, but no extraocular muscle entrapment. Laboratory studies show a TSH of <0.01 μIU/mL (N: 0.5–5.0), free T4 of 4.1 ng/dL (N: 0.8–1.8), and a thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI) level of 320% (N: <140%).
question_stem: In addition to initiating pharmacotherapy for his condition, which of the following is the most appropriate health maintenance intervention to reduce the risk of disease-specific complications in this patient?

answer_choices.A: Bi-weekly complete blood count monitoring for the first six months of therapy
answer_choices.B: Counseling the parents on smoking cessation to reduce the risk of progressive orbitopathy
answer_choices.C: Annual screening for Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus with fasting plasma glucose
answer_choices.D: Obtaining baseline and monthly serum transaminases to monitor for hepatocellular injury
answer_choices.E: Immediate referral for radioactive iodine (I-131) ablation to prevent thyroid storm

explanation: The patient presents with classic signs of Graves disease (hyperactivity, heat intolerance, weight loss, tachycardia, diffuse goiter with bruit, and elevated TSI). In patients with Graves disease, smoking (active or passive) is the strongest modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of Graves ophthalmopathy (orbitopathy). Tobacco smoke increases the severity of orbital inflammation and reduces the effectiveness of treatments for the eye disease. While Graves ophthalmopathy is often milder in children than in adults, smoking cessation (or eliminating second-hand smoke exposure) remains a critical health maintenance intervention to prevent disfiguring or sight-threatening complications. In this case, since the parents smoke indoors, they must be counseled on the direct risk this poses to their child's ocular health.

distractor_explanations.A: While methimazole can cause agranulocytosis (0.2-0.5% of cases), routine CBC monitoring is not recommended. Instead, patients and parents should be instructed to stop the medication and seek immediate medical evaluation for a CBC with differential if a fever or sore throat develops.
distractor_explanations.C: Children with autoimmune thyroid disease have a higher risk of other autoimmune conditions (APS Type 2). However, routine annual screening for Type 1 Diabetes in an asymptomatic child is not standard practice; screening is typically reserved for those with symptoms or if the patient has Type 1 Diabetes (where they are then screened for thyroid disease).
distractor_explanations.D: Hepatocellular injury is a well-known 'Black Box Warning' for propylthiouracil (PTU), which is why PTU is generally avoided in children. Methimazole is the first-line thionamide in children and, while it can rarely cause cholestatic jaundice, routine monitoring of transaminases is not recommended.
distractor_explanations.E: Radioactive iodine (RAI) is a definitive treatment option, but it is typically not the first-line 'immediate' step in an 8-year-old child. Most pediatric endocrinologists begin with a trial of methimazole for 1-2 years to see if remission occurs before considering definitive options like RAI or surgery.

learning_objective: Smoking and second-hand smoke exposure are the most significant modifiable risk factors for the development and progression of Graves ophthalmopathy.
key_concept: Graves disease health maintenance and smoking cessation.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 32-year-old woman presents to an urgent care center complaining of a 15-lb weight gain and "unusual skin markings" over the past 3 months. Her medical history is significant for systemic lupus erythematosus, which has been managed with 40 mg of daily prednisone for the last six months following a severe flare. Physical examination reveals a rounded, plethoric face, a fatty prominence over the dorsocervical region, and central adiposity with relatively thin extremities. Multiple 1.5-cm wide violaceous striae are visible across her abdomen. Her blood pressure is 152/94 mmHg. She also reports difficulty rising from a seated position without using her arms for support. The physician explains that these findings are due to the physiological effects of her current medication.
question_stem: In addition to tapering her medication, which of the following health maintenance interventions is most appropriate to screen for a complication related to the medication’s inhibitory effect on osteoblast activity?

answer_choices.A: Electromyography (EMG)
answer_choices.C: Serial serum creatine kinase (CK) levels
answer_choices.D: Annual slit-lamp examination
answer_choices.E: Periodic 24-hour urinary protein collection

explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of exogenous Cushing syndrome (iatrogenic hypercortisolism) due to chronic high-dose corticosteroid use. Cortisol has widespread catabolic effects. In bone metabolism, glucocorticoids lead to osteoporosis through several mechanisms: they directly inhibit osteoblast proliferation and function (the primary mechanism for decreased bone formation), increase osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, and decrease intestinal calcium absorption (which can lead to secondary hyperparathyroidism). Because glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is a common and debilitating complication, health maintenance guidelines recommend screening with a dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan for patients on long-term, high-dose steroids (typically defined as ≥2.5–5 mg of prednisone for ≥3 months) to assess fracture risk and determine the need for bisphosphonate therapy.

distractor_explanations.A: Electromyography (EMG) is used to evaluate neuromuscular disorders. While this patient has proximal muscle weakness (steroid myopathy), this is a clinical diagnosis characterized by muscle atrophy due to protein catabolism. EMG is not a screening tool and is often normal or shows non-specific findings in steroid myopathy.
distractor_explanations.C: Serum creatine kinase (CK) levels are typically normal in steroid-induced myopathy because the weakness is due to muscle fiber atrophy (catabolism) rather than inflammatory muscle destruction (necrosis), which distinguishes it from inflammatory myopathies like polymyositis.
distractor_explanations.D: Annual slit-lamp examinations are recommended for patients on long-term steroids to screen for posterior subcapsular cataracts and glaucoma. However, the question specifically asks for an intervention related to the medication's effect on osteoblast activity (bone), not the eyes.
distractor_explanations.E: Periodic 24-hour urinary protein collection is used to monitor for lupus nephritis in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus, but it does not screen for the complications of cortisol excess or the specific musculoskeletal mechanisms described.

learning_objective: Recognize that chronic glucocorticoid excess (Cushing syndrome) causes osteoporosis by inhibiting osteoblast activity, necessitating screening with a DEXA scan.
key_concept: Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis and the clinical importance of bone density screening in Cushing syndrome.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old male is evaluated in the clinic for a 2-month history of worsening headaches and progressive "blurry vision" in the periphery. His medical history is significant for Cushing disease, which was refractory to two prior transsphenoidal resections of a pituitary microadenoma. One year ago, due to persistent, severe hypercortisolism, he underwent bilateral adrenalectomy. He has since been maintained on physiologic doses of hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone. On physical examination, there is notable bronze-like hyperpigmentation of the gingival mucosa, palmar creases, and a prior abdominal surgical scar. Visual field testing reveals bitemporal hemianopsia. Laboratory evaluation shows a morning plasma adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) level of 3,200 pg/mL (Reference range: 10–60 pg/mL). A dedicated MRI of the brain reveals a 2.4-cm mass in the sella turcica with significant suprasellar extension and compression of the optic chiasm.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

answer_choices.A: Increase the daily dose of hydrocortisone
answer_choices.C: Pituitary radiation therapy
answer_choices.D: Surgical transsphenoidal resection
answer_choices.E: Observation with serial MRI every 6 months

explanation: This patient presents with Nelson syndrome, a known complication of bilateral adrenalectomy performed for the treatment of refractory Cushing disease. Nelson syndrome occurs when the loss of negative feedback from cortisol (following adrenalectomy) leads to the rapid growth of a pre-existing pituitary corticotroph microadenoma. This results in extremely high levels of ACTH (often >1000 pg/mL) and progressive enlargement of the pituitary gland. Clinical features include hyperpigmentation (due to ACTH cross-reactivity with melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptors) and mass effect symptoms (headaches, bitemporal hemianopsia). The most appropriate management for a symptomatic pituitary macroadenoma (as evidenced by the visual field deficits and size) is surgical transsphenoidal resection to decompress the optic chiasm and reduce the tumor burden.

distractor_explanations.A: While Nelson syndrome is driven by the lack of cortisol-mediated negative feedback, increasing hydrocortisone beyond physiologic replacement doses is not an effective treatment for a growing pituitary macroadenoma and would result in iatrogenic Cushing syndrome.
distractor_explanations.B: Pasireotide is a somatostatin analog approved for Cushing disease (when surgery fails or is not an option), but it is not the first-line treatment for an enlarging pituitary mass causing visual field defects in Nelson syndrome.
distractor_explanations.C: Radiation therapy is a management option for Nelson syndrome, but it is typically reserved for patients who are not surgical candidates, have residual tumor after surgery, or have tumors that are resistant to surgical management. With acute visual field loss, surgical decompression is preferred.
distractor_explanations.E: Observation is inappropriate for a pituitary macroadenoma that is causing symptoms (headaches, bitemporal hemianopsia) and demonstrating rapid growth/high hormonal activity.

learning_objective: Recognize Nelson syndrome as a complication of bilateral adrenalectomy and identify transsphenoidal surgery as the primary treatment for symptomatic pituitary enlargement.
key_concept: Nelson syndrome occurs post-bilateral adrenalectomy due to loss of cortisol feedback, leading to pituitary adenoma growth, high ACTH, and hyperpigmentation.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old female is admitted to the inpatient ward for an expedited workup of refractory hypertension and rapidly progressive weight gain. Physical examination reveals central obesity, a rounded 'moon' facies, and 1.5-cm wide violaceous striae on her abdomen and thighs. Initial screening confirms hypercortisolism with a 24-hour urinary free cortisol level of 450 μg/24h (normal < 50). Her plasma ACTH level is 115 pg/mL (normal 10–60). Following the administration of 8 mg of dexamethasone at midnight, her 8 AM serum cortisol level suppresses from a baseline of 32 μg/dL to 10 μg/dL. A contrast-enhanced MRI of the pituitary gland reveals a 3-mm hypoenhancing lesion in the left lateral wing of the adenohypophysis.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
answer_choices.C: Transsphenoidal resection of the pituitary gland
answer_choices.D: Bilateral adrenalectomy
answer_choices.E: Metyrapone stimulation test

explanation: This patient has confirmed ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome (elevated cortisol and elevated ACTH). The diagnostic challenge in ACTH-dependent Cushing's is distinguishing between a pituitary source (Cushing disease) and an ectopic source (e.g., small cell lung cancer or bronchial carcinoid). The high-dose dexamethasone suppression test (HDDST) shows suppression of cortisol (a >50% drop), which strongly suggests a pituitary source (Cushing disease), as ectopic sources are typically resistant to feedback. However, pituitary microadenomas are frequently too small to be seen on MRI (up to 40% are invisible), and conversely, pituitary 'incidentalomas' (non-functioning lesions) are found in up to 10% of the general population. In cases where the MRI shows a lesion smaller than 6 mm or is entirely negative, inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) is the gold standard next step to confirm that the ACTH is indeed originating from the pituitary before proceeding with neurosurgery.

distractor_explanations.A: CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis is indicated to search for an ectopic ACTH-producing tumor. This is typically the next step if the ACTH were elevated but the cortisol failed to suppress on high-dose dexamethasone.
distractor_explanations.C: Transsphenoidal surgery is the treatment of choice for Cushing disease, but it is generally deferred until the source is definitively localized. Because the MRI lesion is only 3 mm (less than the 6 mm threshold where a lesion is considered likely to be the causative adenoma), IPSS is required to avoid unnecessary or incorrect surgery.
distractor_explanations.D: Bilateral adrenalectomy is a treatment of last resort for refractory Cushing disease (e.g., when pituitary surgery fails or the source cannot be found) or for certain genetic adrenal conditions. It is not an appropriate next diagnostic step.
distractor_explanations.E: The metyrapone stimulation test was historically used to differentiate Cushing disease from ectopic ACTH (Cushing disease responds with an increase in 11-deoxycortisol), but it has largely been replaced by the CRH stimulation test and IPSS in modern clinical practice.

learning_objective: In ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome, if pituitary MRI is negative or reveals a lesion smaller than 6 mm, inferior petrosal sinus sampling is the next best step to differentiate a pituitary source from an ectopic source.
key_concept: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) is the most sensitive test for localizing the source of ACTH production when biochemical tests suggest a pituitary source but imaging is equivocal or shows a very small (<6 mm) lesion.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A female newborn delivered at 26 weeks gestation is currently 4 weeks old and being managed in the neonatal intensive care unit. She was transferred from a community hospital two days ago for escalating respiratory support secondary to bronchopulmonary dysplasia. On physical examination, the infant has a weight-for-age at the 90th percentile, whereas her birth weight was at the 25th percentile. She has notably rounded, full cheeks, a prominent fat pad over the cervicodorsal spine, and fine hair growth over her shoulders and forehead. Examination of the genitalia reveals normal female anatomy without virilization. Her blood pressure is 94/62 mmHg (>95th percentile for age). Laboratory studies show a random blood glucose of 178 mg/dL and a serum potassium of 3.3 mEq/L. The transfer summary from the referring facility mentions 'standard chronic lung disease management' but lacks a specific list of recent medications and dosages.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management to establish the underlying cause of this patient's findings while prioritizing patient safety?

answer_choices.A: 24-hour urine collection for free cortisol
answer_choices.B: Review of the medication administration record from the referring hospital
answer_choices.C: Renal artery duplex ultrasound to evaluate for secondary hypertension
answer_choices.D: Serum 17-hydroxyprogesterone and androstenedione levels
answer_choices.E: Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test

explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of Cushing syndrome: moon facies (rounded cheeks), buffalo hump (cervicodorsal fat pad), hypertension, hyperglycemia, and hirsutism. In the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU), especially in a preterm infant with bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), the most common cause of these findings is iatrogenic Cushing syndrome due to exogenous corticosteroid administration (e.g., dexamethasone used to facilitate extubation or treat BPD). Patient safety and high-value care protocols dictate that medication reconciliation should be performed during transitions of care (such as a hospital transfer) to identify current treatments before embarking on an invasive or expensive diagnostic workup. Reviewing the medication administration record (MAR) is the most efficient and safe way to confirm exogenous steroid use, which would explain the clinical picture without the need for further testing.

distractor_explanations.A: A 24-hour urine free cortisol is a first-line screening test for endogenous Cushing syndrome, but it is technically difficult to perform in a neonate and would be confounded by the likely presence of exogenous corticosteroids.
distractor_explanations.C: While renal artery stenosis is a cause of neonatal hypertension, it does not explain the concurrent findings of moon facies, hirsutism, and hyperglycemia.
distractor_explanations.D: These tests screen for Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH). While 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency can cause hypertension and hirsutism, it typically presents with virilization (ambiguous genitalia) in females and does not cause the fat redistribution seen in Cushing syndrome. Furthermore, 21-hydroxylase deficiency (the most common CAH) typically causes hypotension and salt-wasting.
distractor_explanations.E: A dexamethasone suppression test is used to diagnose endogenous Cushing syndrome. If the patient is already receiving exogenous dexamethasone for BPD, this test is both redundant and physiologically confounded, leading to potential diagnostic error.

learning_objective: Recognize that exogenous corticosteroids are the most common cause of Cushing syndrome in the neonatal ICU and that medication reconciliation is a critical safety step to avoid unnecessary workups for endogenous hypercortisolism.
key_concept: Iatrogenic Cushing syndrome in the NICU is frequently caused by steroids used for BPD; medication reconciliation is the primary step in resolving diagnostic uncertainty during handoffs.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 55-year-old female is admitted to the intensive care unit for acute respiratory failure. She was intubated in the emergency department and started on empiric antibiotics. Bronchoalveolar lavage is performed, and silver staining of the fluid reveals cup-shaped cysts consistent with Pneumocystis jirovecii. An HIV screen is negative, and the patient has no history of organ transplantation or chronic immunosuppressive therapy. Physical examination is notable for a blood pressure of 175/102 mmHg, moon facies, and centripetal obesity. There are multiple 1.5-cm wide violaceous striae across the lower abdomen and extensive ecchymoses on her forearms at peripheral IV sites. Laboratory studies show a serum glucose of 242 mg/dL and a potassium of 3.0 mEq/L.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's underlying condition?

answer_choices.A: Morning plasma ACTH level
answer_choices.C: High-dose dexamethasone suppression test
answer_choices.D: Computed tomography of the chest and abdomen
answer_choices.E: Morning serum cortisol level

explanation: The patient presents with classic features of Cushing syndrome (centripetal obesity, moon facies, wide purple striae, hypertension, and hyperglycemia). The presence of an opportunistic infection (Pneumocystis jirovecii) in an HIV-negative patient without other causes of immunosuppression suggests severe hypercortisolism. The first step in the diagnostic workup of suspected Cushing syndrome is to confirm hypercortisolism using one of three screening tests: 24-hour urinary free cortisol (UFC), late-night salivary cortisol, or the 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Since the patient is in the ICU and likely has a foley catheter, a 24-hour UFC is a practical and highly sensitive choice to begin the biochemical confirmation.

distractor_explanations.A: Morning plasma ACTH level is the second step in the diagnostic algorithm, used only after hypercortisolism has been biochemically confirmed. It helps differentiate between ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic) and ACTH-independent (adrenal) causes.
distractor_explanations.C: The high-dose dexamethasone suppression test is used later in the algorithm to differentiate between a pituitary source (Cushing disease) and an ectopic ACTH source once ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome is confirmed.
distractor_explanations.D: Imaging (such as CT of the chest or abdomen) should not be performed until biochemical evidence of hypercortisolism is established. While the presentation (PJP and severe hypokalemia) strongly suggests an ectopic ACTH-producing tumor (like small cell lung cancer), jumping to imaging before biochemical screening is a common diagnostic error on the USMLE.
distractor_explanations.E: A random morning serum cortisol level is not a recommended screening test for Cushing syndrome because it does not account for the loss of diurnal variation and can be elevated due to the physiologic stress of the patient's current ICU admission (Pseudo-Cushing).

learning_objective: Recognize that the first step in evaluating suspected Cushing syndrome is biochemical confirmation of hypercortisolism with a 24-hour urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, or low-dose dexamethasone suppression test.
key_concept: Biochemical screening for hypercortisolism must precede localization (ACTH levels and imaging) in the workup of Cushing syndrome.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old male presents to an urgent care clinic with a 4-month history of worsening fatigue, generalized weakness, and intermittent abdominal pain. He reports an unintentional 12-lb weight loss over this period and frequent lightheadedness when standing up quickly. He mentions a peculiar new preference for heavily salted foods. His medical history is significant only for vitiligo on his hands and forearms. On examination, his blood pressure is 96/60 mmHg while seated and 82/52 mmHg while standing. Pulse is 102/min and regular. Physical examination is notable for hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases and darkening of a surgical scar on his right knee from a childhood injury. Laboratory studies reveal: Sodium 131 mEq/L, Potassium 5.4 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, and Glucose 74 mg/dL. A complete blood count shows a leukocyte count of 6,200/µL with 9% eosinophils (Normal: <5%).
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis in this patient?

answer_choices.A: 24-hour urinary free cortisol level
answer_choices.C: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
answer_choices.D: Morning plasma ACTH and cortisol levels
answer_choices.E: Pituitary magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)

explanation: This patient presents with classic signs of primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease), including orthostatic hypotension, weight loss, salt craving, and hyperpigmentation (due to increased ACTH and MSH). The presence of vitiligo suggests an autoimmune etiology (Autoimmune Adrenalitis), which is the most common cause in developed countries. Laboratory findings of hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and mild non-anion gap metabolic acidosis support a deficiency in mineralocorticoids (aldosterone), which is characteristic of primary (but not secondary) adrenal insufficiency. Eosinophilia is a classic, though often subtle, hematologic finding in adrenal insufficiency because glucocorticoids normally promote eosinophil apoptosis. The most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis is the cosyntropin (synthetic ACTH) stimulation test. In a normal individual, cosyntropin administration causes a rapid rise in serum cortisol (>18 µg/dL); in patients with primary adrenal insufficiency, the adrenal cortex is damaged and fails to respond to ACTH.

distractor_explanations.A: 24-hour urinary free cortisol is used as a screening tool for Cushing syndrome (hypercortisolism), not for adrenal insufficiency.
distractor_explanations.C: CT scan of the abdomen can help identify the cause of primary adrenal insufficiency (e.g., adrenal calcifications in TB, hemorrhage, or atrophy in autoimmune disease), but it is not a confirmatory test for the diagnosis of the insufficiency itself.
distractor_explanations.D: While morning (8 AM) cortisol and ACTH levels are often part of the initial screening, a cosyntropin stimulation test is the definitive confirmatory test required for diagnosis, especially when clinical suspicion is high. If 8 AM cortisol is extremely low (<3 µg/dL), it is suggestive, but the stimulation test remains the gold standard for confirmation.
distractor_explanations.E: Pituitary MRI is used to investigate secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary failure). This patient has hyperpigmentation and hyperkalemia, which indicate high ACTH levels and mineralocorticoid deficiency, respectively, pointing toward a primary adrenal (not pituitary) problem.

learning_objective: Recognize the clinical presentation of primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) and identify the cosyntropin stimulation test as the gold standard confirmatory diagnostic step.
key_concept: Primary adrenal insufficiency presents with hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia, and hyponatremia; the cosyntropin stimulation test is required for definitive diagnosis.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 72-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic for evaluation of worsening fatigue, unintentional 12-lb weight loss over the past three months, and intermittent nausea. His past medical history is significant for vitiligo and well-controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, the patient appears thin and has generalized hyperpigmentation that is most prominent on the palmar creases and buccal mucosa. His blood pressure is 104/62 mmHg while supine and drops to 88/56 mmHg upon standing. Laboratory studies reveal a serum sodium of 130 mEq/L, potassium of 5.4 mEq/L, and a serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level of 34 μIU/mL (N: 0.5–5.0) with a low free T4 level. The physician considers starting thyroid hormone replacement therapy.
question_stem: To prevent a life-threatening complication, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient before initiating levothyroxine?

answer_choices.A: Measurement of thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin levels
answer_choices.B: Performance of a cosyntropin stimulation test
answer_choices.C: Achievement of glycemic control with an insulin pump
answer_choices.D: Ultrasonography of the thyroid gland
answer_choices.E: Measurement of 24-hour urinary metanephrines

explanation: This patient likely has Autoimmune Polyendocrine Syndrome Type 2 (APS-2, or Schmidt Syndrome), characterized by primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease), autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis), and type 1 diabetes mellitus. He presents with classic signs of Addison disease (hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and orthostatic hypotension) alongside new-onset hypothyroidism. A critical clinical pearl in management is that thyroid hormone increases the metabolic clearance of cortisol. In a patient with undiagnosed or untreated adrenal insufficiency, initiating levothyroxine can acutely precipitate an adrenal crisis by rapidly depleting the already low cortisol reserves. Therefore, adrenal function must be assessed with a cosyntropin (ACTH) stimulation test and treated with glucocorticoids before or concurrently with the initiation of thyroid hormone therapy.

distractor_explanations.A: Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins are used to diagnose Graves' disease (hyperthyroidism). This patient has hypothyroidism (high TSH, low T4), and while thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies might confirm Hashimoto's, they do not prevent the emergency associated with Addisonian crisis.
distractor_explanations.C: While glycemic control is important for his type 1 diabetes, it is not the most immediate priority to prevent a life-threatening complication when Addison disease is suspected and thyroid replacement is planned.
distractor_explanations.D: Thyroid ultrasonography is useful for evaluating nodules but does not aid in the diagnosis of adrenal insufficiency or the prevention of an adrenal crisis triggered by levothyroxine.
distractor_explanations.E: Urinary metanephrines are used to screen for pheochromocytoma (MEN 2 syndrome). While pheochromocytoma can cause orthostasis, it typically presents with hypertension, not hyperpigmentation and hyperkalemia. Furthermore, it is not part of the APS-2 complex.

learning_objective: Recognize that in patients with suspected coexisting primary adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism (APS-2), adrenal function must be assessed and glucocorticoids started before levothyroxine to prevent precipitating an adrenal crisis.
key_concept: Thyroid hormone increases cortisol clearance; starting levothyroxine in an untreated Addisonian patient can trigger an adrenal crisis.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 3-year-old male with a known history of primary adrenal insufficiency is brought to the urgent care clinic by his father due to a 24-hour history of a runny nose, cough, and a temperature of 101.2°F (38.4°C). The patient was diagnosed at 18 months of age following a presentation of fatigue, salt-craving, and hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases and oral mucosa. He is currently managed with daily oral hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone. On physical examination, the child is alert and interactive with moist mucous membranes. His blood pressure is 94/60 mmHg and his heart rate is 105/min. The physician diagnoses a viral upper respiratory infection and provides supportive care recommendations. To ensure the patient's safety and prevent a life-threatening complication, the physician provides specific instructions regarding his chronic medication management.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate instruction to provide the father during this illness?

answer_choices.A: Increase the dose of fludrocortisone until the fever resolves
answer_choices.B: Double or triple the dose of oral hydrocortisone for the next 2-3 days
answer_choices.C: Withhold the hydrocortisone dose if the child develops a decreased appetite
answer_choices.D: Administer a dose of intramuscular epinephrine if the cough worsens
answer_choices.E: Transition to intravenous dexamethasone immediately in the urgent care setting

explanation: This patient has primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) and is experiencing a minor physiologic stressor (a viral illness with fever). In healthy individuals, the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis increases cortisol production during stress. Patients with adrenal insufficiency cannot mount this response and are at high risk for a life-threatening adrenal crisis (hypotension, shock, and altered mental status) if their medication is not adjusted. The standard 'patient safety' protocol for minor illnesses ('stress dosing') is to double or triple the daily dose of oral glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone) for the duration of the illness (typically 2-3 days). Once the illness resolves, the patient returns to their maintenance dose.

distractor_explanations.A: Mineralocorticoid replacement (fludrocortisone) does not typically require adjustment during periods of minor illness or stress. The 'stress response' primarily involves the need for increased glucocorticoids.
distractor_explanations.C: Withholding glucocorticoids during an illness is extremely dangerous and is a common cause of precipitating an adrenal crisis. If the patient is unable to tolerate oral medications due to vomiting, they require parenteral (IM or IV) hydrocortisone, not discontinuation.
distractor_explanations.D: Epinephrine is the treatment for anaphylaxis, not adrenal insufficiency. Adrenal crisis is managed with hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation, not epinephrine.
distractor_explanations.E: Intravenous dexamethasone is used in the management of acute adrenal crisis. Since this patient is currently hemodynamically stable and able to tolerate oral intake, oral 'stress dosing' at home is the appropriate safety measure rather than immediate hospitalization for IV therapy.

learning_objective: Patients with primary adrenal insufficiency must be instructed to double or triple their glucocorticoid dose ('stress dosing') during minor illnesses to prevent the development of an adrenal crisis.
key_concept: Stress dosing of glucocorticoids is a critical safety intervention for patients with adrenal insufficiency during illness, surgery, or trauma.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old male with a 50-pack-year smoking history presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive fatigue, generalized weakness, and a 9-kg (20-lb) weight loss. He also reports intermittent abdominal discomfort and a recent preference for very salty foods. His medical history is significant for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. On examination, he appears thin and chronically ill. His blood pressure is 102/60 mmHg while supine and drops to 84/50 mmHg upon standing. He has noticeable darkening of the skin, particularly over his knuckles, palmar creases, and several old surgical scars on his abdomen. Laboratory studies show a serum sodium of 129 mEq/L, potassium of 5.5 mEq/L, and a random glucose of 68 mg/dL. A chest X-ray reveals a 3-cm hilar mass in the right lung.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management to confirm the suspected underlying endocrine diagnosis?

answer_choices.A: Fluid restriction and measurement of urine osmolality
answer_choices.C: 24-hour urinary free cortisol level
answer_choices.D: Morning serum aldosterone-to-renin ratio
answer_choices.E: CT-guided biopsy of the hilar mass

explanation: The patient presents with classic signs of primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease): fatigue, weight loss, salt craving, hypotension/orthostasis, and hyperpigmentation. The laboratory findings of hyponatremia and hyperkalemia further support this, as mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) deficiency leads to sodium wasting and potassium retention. In this patient, the hilar mass and smoking history suggest metastatic lung cancer as the likely cause of adrenal destruction. The gold standard for confirming adrenal insufficiency is the cosyntropin (synthetic ACTH) stimulation test. A failure of serum cortisol to rise significantly (typically <18 μg/dL) after cosyntropin administration confirms the diagnosis. Primary adrenal insufficiency is distinguished from secondary (pituitary) adrenal insufficiency by the presence of hyperpigmentation (due to high ACTH/MSH) and hyperkalemia (due to aldosterone deficiency; aldosterone is primarily regulated by the RAAS, not ACTH).

distractor_explanations.A: Fluid restriction is the initial management for SIADH. While both SIADH and Addison disease present with hyponatremia in a patient with potential lung cancer (small cell lung cancer often causes SIADH), SIADH is characterized by euvolemia and normal potassium levels. This patient's hypotension and hyperkalemia rule out SIADH.
distractor_explanations.C: A 24-hour urinary free cortisol level is used to screen for Cushing syndrome (hypercortisolism), which would present with hypertension, weight gain, and hyperglycemia, the opposite of this patient's presentation.
distractor_explanations.D: The aldosterone-to-renin ratio is the screening test for primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome), which presents with hypertension and hypokalemia.
distractor_explanations.E: While the hilar mass likely represents lung cancer (the probable primary source for adrenal metastases), the most appropriate next step to confirm the *endocrine* diagnosis and ensure the patient is stable (preventing adrenal crisis) is biochemical confirmation of adrenal insufficiency before invasive procedures.

learning_objective: Differentiate primary adrenal insufficiency from similar conditions like SIADH and secondary adrenal insufficiency using clinical signs (hyperpigmentation) and laboratory findings (hyperkalemia and hypotension), and confirm the diagnosis with a cosyntropin stimulation test.
key_concept: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) involves the loss of both glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids, leading to hyperkalemia and orthostatic hypotension, and elevated ACTH levels which cause hyperpigmentation.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 11-year-old girl is brought to a community health center by her mother for episodes of intense headaches and sweating. Her mother reports that over the past two months, the patient has had multiple episodes where she becomes pale, tremulous, and complains that her heart is 'beating out of her chest.' During the current evaluation, the patient appears anxious. Her blood pressure is 154/98 mmHg and her heart rate is 115/min. Physical examination is notable for moist skin but no other abnormalities. Laboratory testing reveals 24-hour urinary metanephrine levels that are three times the upper limit of normal. An abdominal CT scan shows a 3.2 cm well-circumscribed mass in the left adrenal gland. Surgical resection is planned for next month.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the pharmacologic management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Initiation of propranolol alone
answer_choices.B: Initiation of phenoxybenzamine followed by propranolol
answer_choices.C: Initiation of propranolol followed by phenoxybenzamine
answer_choices.D: Urgent laparoscopic adrenalectomy without medical pretreatment
answer_choices.E: Fine-needle aspiration of the adrenal mass to confirm the diagnosis

explanation: The patient presents with the classic triad of pheochromocytoma: episodic headaches, diaphoresis (sweating), and palpitations, along with paroxysmal hypertension. In children, pheochromocytoma is often associated with hereditary syndromes (e.g., MEN 2, VHL, NF1). The definitive treatment is surgical resection. However, preoperative management is critical to prevent a life-threatening hypertensive crisis caused by the intraoperative release of catecholamines. The standard protocol is to initiate an alpha-blocker (e.g., phenoxybenzamine, a nonselective, irreversible alpha-antagonist) first, typically 7–14 days before surgery. Once adequate alpha-blockade is achieved (evidenced by controlled blood pressure and sometimes orthostatic hypotension), a beta-blocker (e.g., propranolol) can be added to manage tachycardia.

distractor_explanations.A: Initiating a beta-blocker alone is dangerous in patients with pheochromocytoma. Beta-2 mediated vasodilation is blocked while alpha-mediated vasoconstriction remains unopposed, leading to a massive increase in peripheral vascular resistance and a potential hypertensive crisis.
distractor_explanations.C: Alpha-blockade must always precede beta-blockade. Reversing this order risks 'unopposed alpha-stimulation,' which can cause severe, refractory hypertension and heart failure.
distractor_explanations.D: Surgery without preoperative medical stabilization is contraindicated. Tumor manipulation during surgery can cause a massive release of catecholamines, leading to intraoperative hypertensive crisis and arrhythmias.
distractor_explanations.E: Fine-needle aspiration of a suspected pheochromocytoma is strictly contraindicated. The procedure can trigger a massive release of catecholamines, leading to a hypertensive crisis, hemorrhage, or death.

learning_objective: Recognize that preoperative management of pheochromocytoma requires alpha-adrenergic blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) before the initiation of beta-adrenergic blockade to prevent hypertensive crisis.
key_concept: Preoperative 'alpha before beta' blockade for pheochromocytoma management.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 22-year-old female is evaluated in the inpatient ward on post-operative day 2 following an uncomplicated laparoscopic right adrenalectomy. Preoperatively, she presented with a six-month history of paroxysmal headaches, drenching sweats, and a 5-kg weight loss. Her blood pressure, which was previously as high as 190/110 mmHg, is currently 114/72 mmHg without the use of antihypertensive medications. Physical examination shows three well-healing laparoscopic port sites and no cutaneous findings or palpable neck masses. Her family history is significant for a maternal grandmother who died of a 'sudden stroke' at age 48, but there is no known history of endocrine disorders. Histopathology of the resected 3.5 cm adrenal mass confirms a pheochromocytoma with no evidence of capsular or vascular invasion.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to guide long-term health maintenance and screening for this patient?

answer_choices.A: Annual 24-hour urinary metanephrine and catecholamine excretion measurements only
answer_choices.B: Referral for germline genetic testing for RET, VHL, and SDHx mutations
answer_choices.C: Measurement of serum calcitonin and parathyroid hormone levels every six months
answer_choices.D: Serial 123I-metaiodobenzylguanidine (MIBG) scintigraphy every two years
answer_choices.E: Total thyroidectomy as a prophylactic measure against medullary thyroid carcinoma

explanation: Current clinical guidelines recommend that all patients with pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma be offered germline genetic testing, regardless of age, family history, or clinical presentation. Approximately 35-40% of these patients have an underlying germline mutation in one of several susceptibility genes (e.g., RET for MEN2, VHL for von Hippel-Lindau, or SDHx for familial paraganglioma syndromes). Identifying a mutation is critical for health maintenance because it dictates the screening protocols for other potentially lethal associated conditions, such as medullary thyroid cancer (MEN2), renal cell carcinoma (VHL), and extra-adrenal paragangliomas (SDHx). Additionally, genetic testing facilitates cascade testing for family members who may be at risk but asymptomatic.

distractor_explanations.A: While annual biochemical testing is recommended post-operatively to monitor for recurrence or metastasis, it is insufficient for comprehensive health maintenance. It does not address the risk of other associated malignancies (like RCC or MTC) that would be uncovered via genetic testing.
distractor_explanations.C: Measuring calcitonin and PTH levels is appropriate for screening patients with MEN2A. However, genetic testing (RET mutation) should be performed first to establish the diagnosis and necessity of these specific screenings. Furthermore, it doesn't screen for non-MEN2 syndromes like VHL or SDHx.
distractor_explanations.D: Serial MIBG scans are not indicated for routine surveillance of a completely resected, localized pheochromocytoma. MIBG is used primarily for initial localization or when metastatic disease is suspected.
distractor_explanations.E: Prophylactic thyroidectomy is indicated only in patients with confirmed RET proto-oncogene mutations (MEN2). Performing surgery without genetic confirmation in a patient with no neck masses or family history of MTC is inappropriate.

learning_objective: Understand that all patients diagnosed with pheochromocytoma should be offered germline genetic testing to screen for associated hereditary syndromes.
key_concept: Approximately 40% of pheochromocytomas are associated with germline mutations (RET, VHL, NF1, SDHx), necessitating genetic testing for all patients to guide systemic screening and family counseling.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 14-year-old girl is brought to the urgent care clinic for evaluation of recurrent "spells" that began two months ago. Her mother describes episodes characterized by the sudden onset of pounding headaches, profuse sweating, and a racing heart that last for 15 to 30 minutes. The patient's school counselor suggested these might be panic attacks related to upcoming exams. However, the mother notes that the patient often appears unusually pale during these episodes. On examination, the patient's blood pressure is 158/96 mmHg and her heart rate is 105 bpm. Physical examination is notable for several flat, hyperpigmented tan macules on her trunk and axillary freckling. There is no thyromegaly or abdominal masses. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial screening test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?

answer_choices.A: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
answer_choices.C: 24-hour urinary vanillylmandelic acid (VMA)
answer_choices.D: Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH)
answer_choices.E: Renal artery duplex ultrasonography

explanation: The patient presents with the classic triad of pheochromocytoma: episodic headache, diaphoresis (sweating), and palpitations, accompanied by paroxysmal hypertension. The presence of café-au-lait spots and axillary freckling strongly suggests Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), a genetic syndrome associated with pheochromocytoma. In patients with high clinical suspicion (triad) or high-risk genetic syndromes, measurement of plasma free metanephrines is the most appropriate initial screening test due to its very high sensitivity (nearly 99%). Metanephrines are the stable metabolites of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) and are continuously leaked by the tumor into the circulation.

distractor_explanations.A: While a CT or MRI is used to localize the tumor, imaging should only be performed after biochemical evidence of catecholamine excess has been established. Radiologic testing before biochemical confirmation can lead to the incidental discovery of non-secreting adrenal masses (incidentalomas).
distractor_explanations.C: Urinary vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) is a metabolite of catecholamines, but it has lower sensitivity and specificity compared to metanephrines. VMA is more commonly utilized in the workup of neuroblastoma, which is the more common catecholamine-secreting tumor in infants and toddlers, though it usually presents as a firm, fixed abdominal mass.
distractor_explanations.D: Hyperthyroidism can present with palpitations, sweating, and tachycardia. However, it does not typically cause paroxysmal, severe hypertension or the severe pounding headaches seen in this patient.
distractor_explanations.E: Renal artery duplex is used to screen for renal artery stenosis, which is a common cause of secondary hypertension in children and adolescents (e.g., due to fibromuscular dysplasia). While it causes hypertension, it does not explain the paroxysmal triad of headache, sweating, and palpitations.

learning_objective: Recognize the clinical triad of pheochromocytoma and identify plasma free metanephrines as the most sensitive initial screening test, especially in high-risk patients with predisposing genetic syndromes.
key_concept: Pheochromocytoma screening requires biochemical confirmation (metanephrines) prior to anatomical localization (CT/MRI).
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital for the management of a 4.2-cm right-sided adrenal mass discovered during a workup for episodic palpitations and diaphoresis. Her blood pressure on admission is 165/100 mm Hg. Laboratory evaluation reveals a 24-hour urinary metanephrine level of 2,400 μg (normal <600) and normetanephrine of 3,800 μg (normal <900). She is started on phenoxybenzamine to provide adequate alpha-adrenergic blockade prior to planned surgical resection. Over the next four days, the dose is titrated upward. On day 5 of her hospitalization, her blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg while supine and 100/65 mm Hg while standing. Her pulse is 108/min and regular. She reports feeling mild lightheadedness upon standing but is otherwise asymptomatic.
question_stem: Which of the following physiological mechanisms best explains why the medication being used in this patient results in a higher degree of tachycardia compared to selective alpha-1 adrenergic antagonists?

answer_choices.A: Reflexive increase in sympathetic outflow due to peripheral vasodilation
answer_choices.B: Antagonism of presynaptic alpha-2 receptors leading to increased norepinephrine release
answer_choices.C: Covalent inactivation of acetylcholinesterase at the sinoatrial node
answer_choices.D: Upregulation of beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the myocardium
answer_choices.E: Direct agonism of the beta-2 receptors in the peripheral vasculature

explanation: The patient has a pheochromocytoma, for which the standard preoperative management is alpha-blockade followed by beta-blockade. Phenoxybenzamine is the preferred initial agent; it is a non-selective, irreversible alpha-1 and alpha-2 receptor antagonist. While both selective alpha-1 blockers (e.g., doxazosin, prazosin) and non-selective blockers (phenoxybenzamine) cause reflex tachycardia due to vasodilation-induced baroreceptor activation, phenoxybenzamine causes significantly more tachycardia. This is because it also blocks the presynaptic alpha-2 receptors. Normally, presynaptic alpha-2 receptors provide negative feedback to inhibit further norepinephrine (NE) release from the sympathetic nerve endings. By blocking these 'brakes,' phenoxybenzamine allows for increased NE release into the synaptic cleft. This excess NE then stimulates unblocked beta-1 receptors in the heart, exacerbating the tachycardia.

distractor_explanations.A: Reflexive baroreceptor activation occurs with both selective (alpha-1) and non-selective (alpha-1 and alpha-2) antagonists due to the drop in systemic vascular resistance. While it contributes to the tachycardia, it does not explain the *difference* in the degree of tachycardia between the two classes.
distractor_explanations.C: Phenoxybenzamine does not affect acetylcholinesterase; its primary mechanism is the covalent (irreversible) blockade of alpha-adrenergic receptors.
distractor_explanations.D: Upregulation of beta-receptors typically occurs in response to chronic beta-blocker therapy or states of low catecholamine exposure, not acutely following the initiation of an alpha-blocker.
distractor_explanations.E: Phenoxybenzamine is an antagonist, not an agonist. While beta-2 stimulation (by circulating epinephrine from the tumor) causes vasodilation, phenoxybenzamine does not directly act on these receptors.

learning_objective: Understand that non-selective alpha-blockers like phenoxybenzamine cause more pronounced tachycardia than selective alpha-1 blockers because they also antagonize presynaptic alpha-2 receptors, thereby increasing norepinephrine release.
key_concept: The role of presynaptic alpha-2 receptor antagonism in causing tachycardia during the management of pheochromocytoma.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: An 8-year-old girl is brought to the outpatient clinic for evaluation of recurrent episodes of headaches, palpitations, and profuse sweating over the past five months. Her mother reports that during these episodes, the child appears pale and extremely anxious. The patient has no significant past medical history. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 152/98 mmHg (sitting) and heart rate is 114/min. Growth parameters are in the 50th percentile. Laboratory studies show a plasma free normetanephrine level of 2,100 pg/mL (reference: <148 pg/mL) and a plasma free metanephrine level of 48 pg/mL (reference: <57 pg/mL). An MRI of the abdomen is performed, which reveals normal-appearing adrenal glands and no obvious masses in the retroperitoneum.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to localize the source of the patient's symptoms?

answer_choices.A: Renal artery duplex ultrasonography
answer_choices.C: Clonidine suppression test
answer_choices.D: 24-hour urinary vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) measurement
answer_choices.E: CT scan of the head and neck

explanation: This patient presents with the classic triad of pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma (headache, sweating, palpitations) and paroxysmal hypertension. The biochemical diagnosis is confirmed by a significantly elevated plasma normetanephrine level (>3 times the upper limit of normal). A key clinical reasoning point here is the interpretation of the biochemical profile: the patient has isolated normetanephrine elevation with a normal metanephrine level. This suggests the tumor lacks the enzyme phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase (PNMT), which converts norepinephrine to epinephrine. PNMT is primarily found in the adrenal medulla; therefore, its absence strongly suggests an extra-adrenal location (paraganglioma). Because the initial anatomical imaging (MRI of the abdomen) was negative for adrenal masses, functional imaging with 123I-MIBG scintigraphy is the most appropriate next step to localize the tumor elsewhere in the body (e.g., the thorax, pelvis, or other sympathetic chain locations). In children, about 40% of these tumors are associated with genetic syndromes (e.g., SDHx, VHL), and they have a higher incidence of extra-adrenal and multiple lesions compared to adults.

distractor_explanations.A: Renal artery duplex is used to evaluate for renovascular hypertension (e.g., fibromuscular dysplasia), which is a common cause of secondary hypertension in children. however, it does not explain the classic triad of symptoms or the confirmed catecholamine excess.
distractor_explanations.C: The clonidine suppression test is sometimes used in adults when biochemical results are equivocal (borderline elevations). In this case, the normetanephrine level is more than 10 times the upper limit of normal, making the diagnosis certain and the test unnecessary.
distractor_explanations.D: Measurement of VMA has lower sensitivity and specificity compared to plasma or urinary metanephrines. Since the diagnosis is already biochemically confirmed, repeating testing with a less sensitive marker is not indicated.
distractor_explanations.E: While paragangliomas can occur in the head and neck (e.g., carotid body tumors), these are usually parasympathetic and non-secretory. Sympathetic secretory paragangliomas are typically located in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Functional imaging (MIBG) is a more efficient next step than localized CT because it provides whole-body screening for chromaffin tissue.

learning_objective: Understand that isolated normetanephrine elevation in the context of a negative adrenal MRI suggests an extra-adrenal paraganglioma, which is best localized using functional imaging such as an MIBG scan.
key_concept: Extra-adrenal paragangliomas lack PNMT and thus produce normetanephrine/norepinephrine rather than metanephrine/epinephrine; they require whole-body functional imaging (MIBG or DOTATATE) for localization if initial anatomical imaging is negative.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 3-month-old female is brought to the emergency department for worsening pallor and poor feeding over the last week. She was born full-term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery and has been exclusively breastfed. Physical examination reveals a pale, lethargic infant with a heart rate of 172/min and a grade II/VI systolic flow murmur at the left upper sternal border. Abdominal examination is unremarkable with no masses or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 6.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 61 fL, and a red cell distribution width (RDW) of 19%. Iron studies show a serum ferritin of 5 ng/mL and a total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) of 490 µg/dL. Stool guaiac testing is positive for occult blood. Following stabilization with a packed red blood cell transfusion, a technetium-99m pertechnetate scan is performed, which demonstrates a focal area of increased radiotracer uptake in the right lower quadrant that appears simultaneously with the gastric mucosa.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the surgical management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Simple laparoscopic diverticulectomy
answer_choices.C: Ladd procedure with appendectomy
answer_choices.D: Upper endoscopy with bipolar cauterization
answer_choices.E: Nissen fundoplication and gastrostomy tube placement

explanation: The patient presents with severe iron deficiency anemia (IDA) secondary to chronic gastrointestinal blood loss, as evidenced by microcytic anemia, low ferritin, high TIBC, and positive stool guaiac. In a 3-month-old, IDA is pathologic and suggests a structural lesion. The technetium-99m pertechnetate scan (Meckel's scan) showing uptake in the right lower quadrant is diagnostic of Meckel diverticulum containing ectopic gastric mucosa. The ectopic mucosa secretes acid, which causes ulceration of the adjacent, unprotected ileal mucosa. For a bleeding Meckel diverticulum, the most appropriate surgical management is a segmental ileal resection. This ensures that both the diverticulum and the adjacent ileal segment containing the actual bleeding ulcer are removed. Simple diverticulectomy (Choice A) carries a risk of leaving the ileal ulcer behind, leading to recurrent bleeding.

distractor_explanations.A: While simple diverticulectomy (removing only the diverticulum) may be appropriate for an incidentally discovered Meckel diverticulum, it is insufficient for a symptomatic bleeding diverticulum because the ulcerated tissue is typically located in the adjacent ileum, not the diverticulum itself.
distractor_explanations.C: The Ladd procedure is the surgical treatment for malrotation with or without midgut volvulus. While chronic volvulus can cause GI bleeding, the positive Meckel scan specifically identifies ectopic gastric mucosa, pointing to Meckel diverticulum.
distractor_explanations.D: Upper endoscopy is used to treat upper GI bleeds (e.g., Dieulafoy lesions or ulcers). However, the radiotracer uptake in the right lower quadrant indicates a mid-to-lower GI source (ileum) that is beyond the reach of standard upper endoscopy.
distractor_explanations.E: Nissen fundoplication is used to treat severe gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). While GERD can cause esophagitis and occult bleeding, it would not cause focal radiotracer uptake in the right lower quadrant on a Meckel scan.

learning_objective: Recognize that symptomatic Meckel diverticulum causing gastrointestinal bleeding requires segmental ileal resection to ensure the removal of both the diverticulum and the adjacent ulcerated ileal mucosa.
key_concept: Iron deficiency anemia in an infant must be investigated for a structural source of blood loss; Meckel diverticulum is a classic surgical cause requiring resection of the adjacent ileal segment.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 78-year-old male presents to an urgent care clinic complaining of several months of progressively worsening fatigue and mild shortness of breath when walking his dog. His wife notes that he has recently developed a peculiar habit of constantly crunching on ice cubes throughout the day. He has a history of osteoarthritis, for which he takes over-the-counter naproxen daily. Physical examination reveals conjunctival pallor and mild glossitis. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL and a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 72 fL. Further testing reveals a serum ferritin level of 8 ng/mL (Normal: 20–250 ng/mL) and a total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) of 485 µg/dL (Normal: 250–450 µg/dL). A peripheral blood smear shows microcytic, hypochromic erythrocytes and several pencil-shaped cells.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

answer_choices.A: Initiation of oral ferrous sulfate and reassessment in 3 months
answer_choices.B: Gastrointestinal evaluation with colonoscopy and upper endoscopy
answer_choices.C: Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy to evaluate for myelodysplastic syndrome
answer_choices.D: Fecal occult blood testing (FOBT)
answer_choices.E: Measurement of serum vitamin B12 and folate levels

explanation: This patient presents with classic iron deficiency anemia (IDA), evidenced by microcytic anemia, very low ferritin (the most sensitive marker), high TIBC, and pagophagia (pica for ice). In any male or postmenopausal female with iron deficiency anemia, the most critical next step in management is to identify the source of blood loss. In the elderly, gastrointestinal (GI) malignancy is the 'must-not-miss' cause. While his chronic NSAID use (naproxen) increases the risk of peptic ulcer disease, it does not exempt him from the need to rule out colorectal cancer. Therefore, a complete evaluation of the GI tract (colonoscopy and typically an upper endoscopy/EGD) is mandatory regardless of whether the patient is symptomatic or has a clear alternative risk factor for bleeding.

distractor_explanations.A: While oral iron supplementation is the appropriate treatment for IDA, the priority in an elderly patient is diagnosing the underlying cause (e.g., occult malignancy). Initiating treatment without investigating the cause can lead to a delayed diagnosis of cancer.
distractor_explanations.C: Bone marrow biopsy is the gold standard for assessing iron stores but is invasive and rarely necessary because serum ferritin <15-30 ng/mL is highly specific for IDA. While myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) can occur in the elderly, it typically presents with macrocytic or normocytic anemia and would not explain the low ferritin/high TIBC.
distractor_explanations.D: Fecal occult blood testing (FOBT) or fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) is used for colorectal cancer screening in asymptomatic patients. However, if a patient already has iron deficiency anemia, a negative FOBT/FIT is not sufficient to rule out a GI bleed or malignancy; the patient requires a definitive structural evaluation (colonoscopy).
distractor_explanations.E: Vitamin B12 and folate deficiencies cause macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL). This patient has microcytic anemia (MCV 72 fL), making these deficiencies unlikely.

learning_objective: In elderly patients or postmenopausal women, iron deficiency anemia is considered gastrointestinal blood loss until proven otherwise, necessitating a thorough evaluation of the GI tract with colonoscopy and/or endoscopy.
key_concept: Iron deficiency anemia in an elderly male mandates a workup for GI malignancy, even if other causes like NSAID use are present.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 55-year-old female presents to the outpatient clinic reporting a 4-month history of progressive fatigue and a peculiar, persistent craving for chewing ice. She transitioned through menopause five years ago and denies any history of abnormal vaginal bleeding, hematochezia, or melena. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and mild osteoarthritis, for which she takes lisinopril and occasional over-the-counter ibuprofen. Physical examination reveals pale palpebral conjunctivae and brittle, spoon-shaped fingernails. Laboratory results are significant for a hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 74 fL, and a red cell distribution width (RDW) of 17.2%. A serum ferritin level is 9 ng/mL (Normal: 15–150 ng/mL). The patient notes that her last screening colonoscopy was performed six years ago and was reported as normal.
question_stem: Given the physiological regulation of iron balance in this patient, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

answer_choices.A: Initiate oral ferrous sulfate and repeat the hemoglobin level in 3 months
answer_choices.B: Perform fecal occult blood testing (FOBT) to confirm the source of blood loss
answer_choices.C: Perform both a colonoscopy and an upper endoscopy
answer_choices.D: Perform a pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for endometrial pathology
answer_choices.E: Reassure the patient and repeat the ferritin level in 6 months

explanation: This patient has iron deficiency anemia (IDA), as evidenced by microcytic anemia, high RDW, and low ferritin (the most sensitive and specific marker). In postmenopausal women and men, the pathophysiology of iron deficiency is centered on the principle of 'closed-loop' iron homeostasis. Unlike many other minerals, the human body lacks a regulated physiological mechanism to actively excrete excess iron; instead, iron levels are controlled strictly by intestinal absorption (regulated by hepcidin). Therefore, in an adult without an increased physiological demand (like pregnancy), iron deficiency must be assumed to be due to chronic blood loss until proven otherwise. In this demographic, the most common and concerning source is occult gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding from a malignancy (e.g., colon cancer). Even though she had a normal colonoscopy 6 years ago, the development of IDA is a 'red flag' that necessitates a complete evaluation of the GI tract with both colonoscopy and upper endoscopy (EGD), as screening intervals no longer apply in the presence of new clinical symptoms or diagnostic evidence of blood loss.

distractor_explanations.A: While starting oral iron is part of the treatment for IDA, it should never delay the investigation into the underlying cause in a high-risk patient. Treating the anemia without finding the source can lead to a missed diagnosis of early-stage malignancy.
distractor_explanations.B: Fecal occult blood testing (FOBT or FIT) has low sensitivity for detecting intermittent bleeding from a malignancy or polyp. A negative FOBT cannot rule out GI cancer in the setting of established iron deficiency anemia; direct visualization is required.
distractor_explanations.D: While uterine bleeding is the most common cause of IDA in premenopausal women, this patient is five years postmenopausal and specifically denies vaginal bleeding. GI malignancy is a much more likely and life-threatening source that must be prioritized.
distractor_explanations.E: A ferritin of 9 ng/mL is diagnostic of depleted iron stores. Reassuring the patient or delaying workup for 6 months is inappropriate and dangerous, as it ignores the high likelihood of underlying GI pathology.

learning_objective: Recognize that iron deficiency anemia in postmenopausal women or men requires a full gastrointestinal workup (colonoscopy and endoscopy) to rule out malignancy, regardless of previous screening history.
key_concept: Adult iron deficiency is due to blood loss until proven otherwise because the body lacks a regulated iron excretion pathway.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 35-year-old man presents to an urgent care clinic complaining of progressive fatigue and shortness of breath when climbing stairs over the past three months. He has no significant past medical history and takes no daily medications. He reports a balanced diet that includes meat and vegetables. On physical examination, his heart rate is 102/min and blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg. There is notable conjunctival pallor and the fingernails appear flattened. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 74 fL, and an increased red cell distribution width (RDW). His serum ferritin level is 8 ng/mL (Ref: 20–250 ng/mL) and total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) is 480 µg/dL (Ref: 250–450 µg/dL).
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to evaluate the underlying cause of this patient's anemia?

answer_choices.A: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
answer_choices.C: Colonoscopy
answer_choices.D: Serum lead level
answer_choices.E: Oral ferrous sulfate supplementation only

explanation: This patient has iron deficiency anemia (IDA), characterized by microcytic anemia (low Hb, low MCV) and low ferritin (the most sensitive marker for depleted iron stores). In adult men and postmenopausal women, IDA is considered to be caused by gastrointestinal (GI) blood loss until proven otherwise. Even in a young patient (35 years old) who denies overt blood in the stool (melena or hematochezia), the discovery of IDA warrants a thorough evaluation of the GI tract (colonoscopy and often upper endoscopy) to screen for malignancy, polyps, or angiodysplasia. Diet is rarely the sole cause of IDA in men in developed countries unless there is significant malabsorption.

distractor_explanations.A: Hemoglobin electrophoresis is used to diagnose thalassemias. While thalassemias cause microcytic anemia, they typically present with a normal or elevated ferritin level and a normal TIBC, unlike the findings in this patient.
distractor_explanations.B: Fecal occult blood testing (FOBT) or fecal immunochemical testing (FIT) is used for routine colorectal cancer screening in asymptomatic average-risk individuals. However, in a patient who already has confirmed iron deficiency anemia, a single negative FOBT is insufficient to rule out a GI source of bleeding; direct visualization via colonoscopy is required.
distractor_explanations.D: Lead poisoning can cause a microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling on the peripheral smear, but it would not explain the low ferritin and high TIBC seen in iron deficiency.
distractor_explanations.E: While oral ferrous sulfate is the appropriate first-line treatment for iron deficiency, initiating treatment without investigating the underlying cause is a common clinical error. In a male patient, the source of the iron loss must be identified to ensure a malignancy or other chronic bleeding source is not missed.

learning_objective: Any adult male or postmenopausal female with iron deficiency anemia must undergo a gastrointestinal evaluation (such as colonoscopy) to screen for occult malignancy.
key_concept: Unexplained iron deficiency anemia in a male requires evaluation for gastrointestinal blood loss, regardless of the patient's age or dietary habits.
subject: Internal Medicine

vignette: A 48-year-old male is admitted to the inpatient ward for the management of a severe flare of rheumatoid arthritis. He reports several months of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnea but denies hematochezia, melena, or changes in bowel habits. He has not seen a physician in five years. His current medications include methotrexate and naproxen. Physical examination reveals a pale appearing male with significant swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 75 fL, and a red cell distribution width (RDW) of 17.5%. Serum iron is 30 µg/dL (N: 60–170), total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) is 245 µg/dL (N: 240–450), and ferritin is 92 ng/mL (N: 20–250). C-reactive protein is significantly elevated at 48 mg/L (N: <5). Fecal occult blood testing is negative.
question_stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's anemia?

answer_choices.A: Hemoglobin electrophoresis
answer_choices.C: Reassurance and follow-up in six months
answer_choices.D: Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy
answer_choices.E: Erythropoietin level measurement

explanation: This patient presents with a microcytic anemia (MCV <80) and labs that are somewhat equivocal between anemia of chronic disease (ACD) and iron deficiency anemia (IDA). Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant; in the setting of active inflammation (suggested by his RA flare and high CRP), a 'normal' ferritin level (typically up to 100 ng/mL) does not rule out concurrent iron deficiency. An RDW >15% and a ferritin <100 ng/mL in an inflammatory state are highly suggestive of IDA. In a 48-year-old male, the most common and concerning cause of iron deficiency is occult gastrointestinal blood loss, often due to malignancy or chronic NSAID use (he takes naproxen). Current guidelines recommend colonoscopy starting at age 45 for all individuals; however, in a male with new-onset iron deficiency, a diagnostic colonoscopy (and often an EGD) is mandatory to rule out colorectal cancer, regardless of a negative fecal occult blood test.

distractor_explanations.A: Hemoglobin electrophoresis is used to diagnose thalassemias. While thalassemia causes microcytosis, it typically presents with a normal RDW, a normal or high RBC count (Mentzer index <13), and normal iron studies.
distractor_explanations.C: Reassurance is inappropriate because iron deficiency in an adult male is a 'red flag' that requires investigation for an underlying source of blood loss, particularly malignancy.
distractor_explanations.D: Bone marrow aspiration with Prussian blue staining is the gold standard for assessing iron stores, but it is invasive and rarely necessary. Clinical practice dictates ruling out the most common and dangerous causes of iron loss (GI bleed/cancer) via endoscopy first.
distractor_explanations.E: Erythropoietin levels are used in the workup of anemia of chronic kidney disease or to evaluate polycythemia, but they do not help differentiate between IDA and ACD or identify the source of the anemia.

learning_objective: Recognize that ferritin levels up to 100 ng/mL can still indicate iron deficiency in the setting of inflammation, and that iron deficiency in an adult male requires a workup for gastrointestinal malignancy.
